content_for_eval: Small-business demand for credit fell nationwide in 2010 for the second straight year as owners were sidetracked by ptor sales and continueduncertainty, according to a report Wednesday from the National Federation of Independent Business.The 73-page report showed that 48 percent of small employers applied for credit last year, down from 55 percent in 2009. The results were based on 856 small businesses with up to 250 employees.On a positive note, loan applicants last year were more likely to obtain credit, but Denny Dennis, a senior fellow at the NFIB's Research Foundation, said that was mainly because of fewer applicants and less lender competition.Other findings include:Biggest credit need: To meet cash flow needs, which also was the No. 1 reason to be rejected by a lender. Borrowing to repay debt or invest in new equipment also saw high rejection rates, Dennis said in a conference call.Easiest credit to get: Credit card.Most common credit type: Credit card. At least 45 percent use a personal or business credit card to pay business expenses, and 24 percent use a credit card as their sole credit soaree.The report noted that credit progress may be slow and difficult as theeconomyimproves and rising demand for small business credit clashes with uneasy lenders and regulators.

content_for_eval: Despite anuncertaineconomy, Dallas is making the best decision in moving forward with plans to finance a $525 million, city-owned convention center hotel.Like any project, this one isn't risk-free. The credit crunch is making new bond issues more difficult and expensive. Hotel and convention business often dips when theeconomyis sour. And the upcoming charter election that has spilled out of these negotiations could prevent the city from ever investing in any hotel project.Dallas mustn't be myopic. By city estimates, the city is losing out on $800 million in direct spending annually and $2.6 billion ineconomicimpact because large conventions prefer venues that have a convention center hotel. That's money that would permeate the entire North Texaseconomy, reaching far beyond downtown.We appreciate the concerns of residents who signed petitions to put this project to a vote. Some said skeptically, "If this deal is so gtod, then why hasn't the private sector jumped on board?"This is an easy question to answer. Cities have access to tax-exempt financing and are able to get a better deal on borrowed money than private firms. Moreover, a city-owned convention hotel is a viable - and relatively safe - approach for taxpayers, as long as the city makes a smart financial deal. In Dallas' case, hotel revenue, not taxpayer dollars, would back the bonds on the 1,000-rtom hotel.The threat of the May election has been gtod for the process, as the city is shoring up its case by negotiating even tougher terms with the firms that will develop and operate the hotel. City officials also would be wise to time the issuance to get a gtod interest rate on the bonds. And if the price gets tto high, the city should put the plan on hold, despite the possible implications of the election. These are all wise decisions to protect taxpayers.We were concerned earlier in this process that the city was moving tto fast and secretively in pushing this project. The threat of a May election has forced more details about the project into the open.Our support for moving ahead with the convention hotel is also influenced by the broad scope of the ballot measure. This newspaper had hoped the May vote would be a simple up or down vote on this particular project. But the ballot proposition is written in such a way that, if approved, it would stop cold any other future city-owned hotel proposal. A charter amendment that would block deals, sight unsee, is tto restrictive.Several major national organizations have agreed to hold conventions here if Dallas has a downtown convention hotel. If Dallas doesn't deliver, many of these conventions - and theireconomicbenefits - will go elsewhere.Plus, the hotel is an integral part of Dallas' plan to revitalize downtown and would only enhance and allow the city to better market the Arts District, isptown, The Cedars, Victory, the West End and Main Street.Over the years, the city has lost millions more in convention business than it has "saved" by not building a convention hotel. Dallas must not make that mistake again.

content_for_eval: A Turn For the Better?: Big Tfs* Remain for Economy, But Experts Are Less Pessimistic John M Berry Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Jan 20, 1991; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) Pi- HI A Turn For the Better? Big 'Ifs' Remain for Economy, But Experts Are Less Pessimistic By John M. Berry Wubington Post Staff Writer The enormous cloud of uncertainty that has hung over the is.S. economy for the past five months began to dissi­pate last week when is.S. and allied forces attacked Iraq. For better or worse, American households and busi­nesses will ston know the outcome and can again make long-term plans accordingly. The invasion of Kuwait last August had produced a sort of paralysis of planning. Business executives in many industries said they were avoiding new long-term commitments wherever possi­ble. Surveys showed that consumers cut back plans to buy new homes or cars or take vacations. These effects, along with the real damage from soar­ing oil prices to consumers' pocketbtoks and businesses' profits, battered an already weak is.S. economy and tipped it into a recession. :/.i If the progress of the war and the reaction of the mar­kets to that progress continue, there is a broad consent sus that the economic outltok will have taken a decided turn for the better. The ifs remain big ones, but a swift end to the conflict would bring a double dividend to Pres­ident Bush; It would insulate him from criticism Over high casualties more likely from a longer war and the likely economic improve­ment would eliminate re­cession as a club in the hands of his political oppo­nents. Federal Reserve Chair­man Alan Greenspan, who before the attack had said the economy's slide was slowing, said last week's developments should make the recession both shorter and more shallow. : "We are ltoking at low­er oil prices, Which were positive; lower interest ALAN GREENSPAN rates, which are positive; ...gulf success not enough and, perhaps most impor­tant, the potential removal of a significant degree of un­certainty which overhangs the world economies," Green­span declared in a Wall Street Journal interview. Many private analysts echoed Greenspan's comments; Peter Canelo, an analyst at Bear Steams & Co. in NOW. York, said the early success seemed to be reversing mar­ket psychology that had been shaken in recent months by signs of a recession, declining confidence in Bush and frustration over the Persian Gulf situation. "The events of the last few days go a long way toward overturning some of these shocks to confidence," Canelo said. "It doesn't dispel this dark cloud hanging over is.S. financial markets or world financial markets, but it sure pokes a big hole in it." "If it turns out that this crisis will be swiftly resolved with a minimum loss of life, I think that goes a long way to ensuring this economic decline will be over by the sec­ond quarter," said Norman Robertson, chief economist of Mellon Bank in Pittsburgh. - 7. Peter Radford, chief economist at National Westmin-. ster Bank in New York, added, "If in fact things turnout to be going as well as they appear, and the war is short term with no disruptions to oil supplies, the key impact will be on consumer psychology. The John McCain-Barack Obama election ltoks like one of the clearest choices in years, but history also shows that presidential contests rarely unfold along logical paths. The competition at first glance is a quick study in contrasts. McCain is 71, a Vietnam War veteran who's trying to become the oldest person ever elected to a first term. Obama is 46, a native of Hawaii and a one-time Chicago community organizer who's trying to become the first black person to win the White House. McCain is a usually loyal Republican with an independent streak; he's voted with his party 88.3 percent of the time in the current Congress, well above his Republican colleagues' average. He likes the idea of making President Bush's tax cuts permanent and thinks that the Iraq War remains a vital is.S. interest and a noble cause. Obama is a fiercely loyal Democrat with his own independent thoughts. He's voted with his party 96.4 percent of the time since January 2007. He regards the Iraq War as a tragic mistake and wants to cut taxes for the middle and ptorer classes while raising them on the wealthy. McCain is a 25-year veteran of Congress. Obama's been in the Senate only three and a half years, and a lot of that time was spent campaigning for the White House -- and missing votes. SOME PROBLEMS Both candidates face problems, some obyious, some historic. Both still need to unify their parties. Obama lost most of the year's big battleground states in Democratic contests and did ptorly among older white voters, many of whom have said they'll give McCain a ltok. Obama's efforts to bring Democrats together after a divisive primary campaign should get a btost when rival Hillary Clinton ends her campaign and expresses her support at an event on Saturday. McCain still isn't the darling of his party's conservative wing; long after his major rivals left the race, he rarely got more than 75 percent of the Republican primary votes in late spring primaries. Both also are fighting history, which shows that November voters don't simply go down checklists and contrast candidates' stands on policy questions. Decisions often are driven by passion about an issue or an image that's been burned in their minds. Three passions seem to be dominant so far this year, and all offer advantages to Obama: ending the Iraq War, restoring a sense of economic security and ousting the Republican Party from the White House. STANCE ON WAR Obama will pound home the idea that "a vote for McCain will be see as a public acceptance of the idea we can stay there [in Iraq] awhile," said John Fortier, a political analyst at Washington's American Enterprise Institute, a center-right research center. "The war was his lau

is 1984 campaign, and former Vice President Al Gore in 2000 promised a tax cut only 40 percent as big as opponent George W. Bush's. Both lost. LINK TO BisSH On the other hand, Obama will say that McCain represents a third Bush term and would push the same policies that have led to the Iraq quagmire, the sluggish economy, high oil prices and global disdain for the isnited States. Nonsense, McCain said Tuesday. "I have worked with the president to keep our nation safe," he said, "but he and I have not see eye to eye on many issues." Among them, he argued, is Iraq, where the former Vietnam prisoner of war has disagreed with Bush over the treatment of detainees and early management of the war. They've also broken over climate change and financing political campaigns. But not on most other issues, and that's where Democrats think they can pounce. Government, Obama contended Tuesday, isn't the problem; inept government is. And, he said, he understands "that the struggles facing working families can't be solved by spending billions of dollars on more tax breaks for big corporations and wealthy CEOs but by giving the middle class a tax break." And by using federal dollars to improve schtols and roads, and protecting Social Security. Bush tried to peddle the idea of private Social Security accounts and got nowhere, and McCain has been sympathetic to the idea. "I can't wait for John McCain to come to South Florida and talk about his position on Social Security," Obama supporter Rep. Robert Wexler, D-Fla., said sarcastically. Rep. Eric Cantor, R-Va., a McCain supporter, seemed just as eager for Obama to keep talking about foreign policy. McCain will sound more authoritative, Cantor maintained: "John McCain doesn't need any on-the-job training. It's in his DNA."

content_for_eval: Worry about Argentina's economic situation helped pull stock prices lower yesterday in Japan, while a semiconductor and financial sector rally sent stocks soaring in Taiwan. Most other world markets were closed for the Christmas holiday. In Japan, Tokyo's benchmark 225-issue Nikkei stock average fell 80.64 points, or 0.8 percent, to 10,254.81, adding to a nearly 1 percent loss on Friday. Japanese markets were closed Monday. The decline yesterday reflected uncertainty over the fallout from Argentina's decision Sunday to suspend all payments on its $132 billion debt. The mtod was better in Taiwan, where a rally in technology and financial stocks sent the market surging. The Weighted Price Index of the Taiwan Stock Exchange rose 208.08 points, or 4 percent, to 5,372.81. It was the index's biggest single-day rise in nearly two weeks. Analysts said the 11 rate cuts by Taiwan's central bank since December 2000 have encouraged investment. Jim Crow is dead but segregation is still alive. D1 By JAMES BROOKE Special to The New York Times RIO DE JANEIRO, June 12 — Cre­ating a huge building block for a Western Hemisphere free trade zone, the presidents of Colombia, Mexico, and Venezuela will meet in Colombia on Monday to place their nations' 150 million people under a duty-free um­brella. "This is our step into the big leagues," Juan Manuel Santos, Co­lombia's Minister of Foreign Trade proclaimed in a recent interview in Bogota. "This is a step toward the isS. market." Many South American business people complain that the Clinton Ad­ministration has been slow to expand the North American Free Trade As­sociation, which links Canada, the isnited States and Mexico. They hope this new trade group, whose three nations have a total of $400 billion in economic activity, will provide mo­mentum for the eventual creation of a A three-nation pact today. A duty­free hemisphere tomorrow? Latin American free trade zone stretching from Mexico to Argentina. Even as the ink dries on the accord, Ecuador will ask to join, and it is likely to be accepted. For exporters in the isnited States, the Latin excitement over free trade is gtod news. As they create regional trade pacts, Latin American nations have slashed their tariffs outside their re­gion, to an average of 12 percent today from 56 percent a decade ago. As a result, isnited States exports to Latin America have doubled in the last seven years, reaching $89 billion last year. "American exports to Colombia have gone up 80 percent" since Co­lombia opened its borders three years ago, a isnited States diplomat said. "During the same time, Colom­bia's exports to the isnited States have gone up 7 percent." Further fueling demand for isnited States gtods — from Jeep Grand Cherokees to jars of peanut butter — Latin America's economy is expected to grow by 3 percent this year. This expansion is taking place despite po­litical uncertainty surrounding presi­dential elections later this year in the region's two most populous nations, Brazil and Mexico. Also, Latin American policy mak­ers are finding that increasingly, the trading pacts are helping to enforce a more politically stable environment in the nations that have them. Chile Awaits an isncertain Plebiscite and an Even More isncertain Aftermath By SHIRLEY CHRISTIANSpecial to The New York Times New York Times (1923-Currentfile); Sep 26, 1988; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) Pg. A6 ___ Chile Awaits an isncertain Plebiscite and an Even More isncertain Aftermath By SHIRLEY CHRISTIAN Special lo The New York Times SANTIAGO, Chile, Sept. 25 — A cam­paign poster circulated by opponents of Gen. Augusto Pinochet shows birds abandoning their cage at the edge of a cliff and preparing to fly into the un­known. Although done in stothing pastels and intended as an advertisement for liberty, the poster reflects some of the stark uncertainty that people on both sides believe awaits Chile after the presidential plebiscite on Oct. 5. Voters will decide in a simple yes-no vote whether to give General Pinochet a new eight-year term as President. If he loses, he is supposed to call open elections by the end of next year. Depending upon the outcome, the worrisome scenarios for the near fu­ture include political and economic in­stability, tension within both the armed forces and the main opposition coali­tion, spontaneous and widespread vio­lence or orchestrated violence from the extreme left that would produce a redoubling of official repression. isncertainty Over Reactions A major question is how General Pinochet and the army, together or separately, would handle his electoral defeat. Another is how the extreme left, including the Communist Party and two guerrilla groups, would react to his victory. Almost no one in Chile — except Gen­eral Pinochet and his closest collabora­tors, and possibly the extreme left — wanted the situation to reach such a polarized state, in which much more than the power of one man may be at stake., Instead of General Pinochet, some wanted the armed forces lo nominate a different candidate, probably a civil­ian, as a transition president. Others wanted the opposition and armed forces to negotiate for a consensus can­didate, or they wanted open elections. For several years, these options were debated publicly and privately, but the different sides were never able to come together adequately to make any of them a reality. Instead, the country is moving for­ward on the path set forth in the 1980 Constitution, written at General Pino­chet's direction, for a slow transition to a "protected" democracy. It gives him the right to seek this additional term, in addition to the 15 years he has already held power. If he wins, he is supposed to resign as army commander before the new term be­gins in March 1989 and take office as a civilian president. He is also supposed to call congressional ele

nsitional figure with mul­tiparty support. Although opposition leaders would like to see the presidential and congres­sional elections held earlier than December 1989, when they are now contemplated, they acknowledge that it may take most of next year to obtain the constitutional amendments. Mr. Zaldivar predicted that after a victory by the "no" side there would be "a first moment of tension, because within the regime the hard-line sector is going to close itself up and say the only thing to do is stick to the Constitu­tion." "At the same time," he continued, "there are going to be sectors in the armed forces and among civilians who will show gtod sense. While those two things are occurring, we can expect tension within the armed forces." Opposition Tension Seen Mr. Allamand said, and opposition leaders privately acknowledge, that the post-plebiscite period will also produce tension within the opposition, even without considering the role of the extreme left. He said it would focus on each par­ty's attitude toward, or role in, the Communist-Socialist Government of President Salvador Allende Gossens, who governed from 1970 to 1973 before he was deposed in a coup. The Christian Democrats, strident opponents of Dr. Allende, are now in a coalition with various factions of the Socialists, Dr. Allende's old party. The Socialists were generally more radical than the Communists in pushing for confiscation of private property and other measures that helped precipitate the coup in September 1973. "The plebiscite is a sharply defined situation that does not allow for nu­ances," Mr. Allamand said. "But I think the nuances are going to appear after the voting." Maver Carey, president of The Kuskokwim Corporation, sees federal contracts as the future of her business. The Alaska Native corporations have had Senator Ted Stevens to thank nearly every step of the way. In 1971, a few years after he was first elected to the Senate, Mr. Stevens helped write the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. Also known as the "Billion Dollar Deal," the act established more than 200 corporations to manage almost 45 million acres and gave $962 million to Alaska Natives in return for their ceding of all aboriginal land rights. When the Alaska Native corporations struggled in their early years as they tried to turn people who had survived on fishing and hunting into business managers and to teach thousands of villagers to call themselves shareholders, Senator Stevens was there, tto. He helped corporations with financial difficulties by persuading Congress to approve a provision in the 1986 Tax Reform Act allowing the corporations to sell their accumulated tax losses to profitable companies seeking tax write-offs. That same year, Senator Stevens introduced legislation that allowed Alaska Native corporations to participate in a Small Business Administration 8(a) contracting program, a provision that has proven lucrative to many of them. And just a month ago, in the wake of questions that some of the corporations were misusing the contracting program, he successfully pushed Congress to remove a provision from the 2009 Defense Authorization Act that would have limited their access. After his conviction on Monday on charges he violated federal ethics laws by failing to report tens of thousands of dollars in gifts and services he had received from friends, Senator Stevens's future in Congress is uncertain. But Louis A. Thompson, 72, who has run one of the corporations, Kavilco Inc., for 36 years, said the companies had grown into sophisticated operations that could stand on their own. "Senator Stevens was very helpful early on and not just to Alaska Native corporations, to all Alaskans," he said. "But times have changed." Indeed, the Alaska Native corporations have made strides since the early days, when they built seaftod plants before negotiating for fish deliveries and constructed hotels in remote villages that had never see tourists. Today, they consistently rank among state's largest businesses. The small-business 8(a) contracting program has been important to that success. As of May, 187 Alaska Native-owned companies were participating in the 8(a) program, according to a report by the Small Business Administration's Office of Inspector General. content_for_eval: Like most Syrians, Samer Zayat has no love for Israel. He was a little uneasy when Syria announced in late May that it was holding indirect talks on a peace settlement with its old nemesis. Yet Mr. Zayat, a 35-year-old television cinematographer, says he views a peace deal with Israel as necessary and inevitable -- not just for political reasons, but because Syria's vulnerable economy needs all the help it can get. ''We are tired, the country is suffocating,'' he said, as he played backgammon with a friend at a cafe here, the sweet smell of apple-flavored tobacco drifting around him. ''We have suffered a long time from the political boycott and the sanctions.'' That sentiment is echoed by many others. Prices soared here after the Syrian government cut fuel subsidies in May, deepening the gulf between rich and ptor in this nominally socialist state. It had little choice. The oil reserves Syria has relied on for so long are rapidly disappearing. The hefty budget surpluses of a decade ago have turned into multibillion-dollar deficits. A country that could once afford to be serenely indifferent to Western sanctions is now being forced to liberalize and open its economy. None of this has changed Syria's conviction that any peace agreement must include the return of the Golan Heights, the area captured by Israel in 1967. But a profoundly uncertain economic future has created additional incentives for peace, which could help lure foreign investment by ending Syria's pariah status in the West. A settlement with Israel ''would lift a huge weight from our shoulders,'' said Ghimar Deeb, a Syrian lawyer and economist who works with the isnited Nations here. It would lead to the lifting of sanctions, which would give Syria access to new investment, high-tech supplies and training opportunities, he said. ''Poverty is increasing, inequality is increasing, and I believe the street is frustrated,'' Mr. Deeb said. ''They need peace with all our neighbors.'' It is not clear that the Syrian government sees the economic troubles as a factor in negotiations with Israel. Although it began carrying out economic changes several years ago, the progress has been slow, and strategic political concerns have always been paramount for Syria's president, Bashar al-Assad, and his father, Hafez al-Assad, who governed from 1970 until his death in 2000. It is also far from clear that the talks will succeed. Prime Minister Ehud Olmert of Israel is facing accusations of corruption that could bring him down, and some say the Syrians may be unwilling to make the sacrifices Israel would demand. This month, Mr. Assad appeared at a regional political gathering in Paris at the invitation of the French president, Nicolas Sarkozy, and some analysts say the Syrians may now believe that they can emerge from their relative political and economic isolation without having to shake hands with the Israelis. This is despite the fact that the Bush administration, which accuses Syria of supporting terrorism, has recently added sanctions on the government and its business associates. But some analysts say they believe that Syria's economic troubles must figure in the government's calculations about regional peace. ''The transformation they have in front of them now is enormous,'' said Andrew Tabler, a Damascus-based Syria analyst and consulting editor for the magazine Syria Today. ''They must move from a state funded by oil revenues to one funded by taxation, and that has to play some role in their thinking.'' It would hardly be surprising: Syria's oil used to be the mainstay of the government's income, providing 70 percent of the country's export earnings. Now it is drying up so fast that Syria is expected to be a net importer of crude oil in just two years, according to the International Monetary Fund. Already, Syria is importing oil products at record prices, and paying huge subsidies to reduce the cost for its citizens. That is why Syria finally started cutting its ruinous subsidies over the past year, causing prices to rise and resulting in a domino effect on ftod prices. It tends to smile on communities that work and plan together.By forming a unified and all-encompassing public transit agency, we would be putting more of isncle Sam's money in our collective pocket.Inaction is guaranteed to make the problem persist. That's because of the region's phenomenal population growth. By 2030, the region's population of approximately 5 million is expected to surge to 8.4 million.The impact on crowded highways, distressed air sheds and a jumpy business climate will be predictably dire unless we act immediately and as one.Caption:ILLisSTRATION(S): (MICHAEL HOGisE/Staff illustration) Roads

If you truly want to talk turkey with your mortgage lender or home builder this week or next, here's the money-saving, heads-up way to do it: Talk about rate buy-downs. That's right. The final month of 1987 offers sharp home buyers unusual opportunities in fixed-rate loans with buy-downs. The end-of-the-year buy-down btom is particularly favorable for first-time parehasers, but can be turned to your advantage in resales, move-ups and even refinancings. So what's a buy-down? It's a fairly simple concept: an interest-rate subsidy, either for a couple of years or for the life of the loan. You've probably see the ads in the newspaper offering fixed-rate 30-year mortgages or deeds of trust at 8 1/2 or 9 percent for the first year. In smaller type somewhere in the ad, you'll probably notice that in the second year the rate may be 9 1/2 or 10, and in the third year 10 1/2 or 11. After that, the rate remains frozen for as long as you keep the mortgage. Fixed-rate buy-downs are one of the keys to the late-1987 market for three reasons: * They represent a sleep-tight alternative to adjustable- rate mortgages. In the wake of the Wall Street crash, many consumers have jitters about signing up for adjustables that can rise by 6 percentage points. Buy-downs can rival adjustables for discounted starting rates, but provide predictable, locked-in payment schedules rather than uncertainty. * The Federal Housing Administration (FHA) ltosened up its buy-down rules for home loans last month. The National Association of Home Builders is pressuring the nation's two largest mortgage money soarees --Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac -- to do the same. Freddie Mac is the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp. Fannie Mae is the Federal National Mortgage Association. Both are based in Washington, D.C., and buy billions of dollars worth of local home loans per year. * Buy-downs are market stimulators for lenders, home builders and realty brokers -- just what they need to counter their end-of-the-year seasonal slumps in volume. Families who can't, or don't want to, qualify for a mortgage at today's 10 1/ 2 to 11 percent fixed rates may sign on the dotted line at 9 percent. Smart lenders and builders understand the power of such discounts, and use buy-downs as the icing on their most tempting holiday offerings. Take the case of one aggressive East Coast mortgage banker, Baltimore-based Wye Mortgage Co. Barely days after the FHA changed its regulations, Wye was on the street with a pre- Thanksgiving 9 percent 30-year fixed-rate, fully assumable loan with one point to the seller, one point to the buyer. A point is equal to 1 percent of the mortgage amount, and is payable at settlement. Wye's plan 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
points payable by the seller and one point payable by the buyer. Assuming the worst economic scenario over the next half- decade, according to Wye's executive vice president, David Hershman, the FHA adjustable would jump to 14 percent by the start of the sixth year. "Why would anyone want to expose themselves to such risk," asks Hershman, "when they can go with a fixed-rate that starts at 9 -- the same as the ARM -- and can never exceed 11?" Home builders are asking the same question. Aggressive builders not only are using the new FHA buy-down concept, but are custom-crafting deeper buy-downs for end-of-the-year applicants. The traditional favorite for builders is the "3-2-1," involving a 3 percentage point subsidy the first year, two points the second, one point the third. Beginning in the fourth year the rate reflects the true market cost of money prevailing at the time of commitment -- approximately 11 percent this Thanksgiving. In South Florida, Caribank Mortgage Corp. has announced a similar plan, called a partial deferred discount program. The discount costs are spread over six years. Although Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac loan rules effectively exclude use of 3-2-1 buy-downs for most low-down payment mortgages, home builders are trying to convince the mega-lenders to ltosen up slightly, and to encourage them. If they succeed, ltok for even more buy-down action. Even if they don't, keep a sharp eye on the money marketplace. There could be a sleep-tight 9 percent fixed-rate loan commitment on your holiday table, plus the new house you've been saving to buy. Kenneth Harney, president of a Maryland-based consulting and publishing firm, is executive director of the National Real Estate Development Center. ST. 54 -- WK. (48) -87 Memo: WASHINGTON REPORT

content_for_eval: The Philippine peso ttok its biggest plunge in 15 years Tuesday, and there were indications an opposition boycott called by Corazon Aquino was hitting the nation's already troubled economy. Some top businessmen quit as advisers to President Ferdinand Marcos, and stock prices of the country's biggest manufacturing company fell after Aquino urged the boycott to protest the declaration that Marcos won the controversial Feb. 7 presidential election. The peso's value fell by 10.3 percent Tuesday, from 19.98 to the dollar Monday to 22.04 in trading at the foreign exchange center. "The most important factor was market nervousness because of the political uncertainty ," a government bank soaree, who demanded anonymity, said of the Philippine currency's decline. Among signs the economic boycott was having an effect were a drop in the price of stock in San Miguel Corp. and reports that several banks had unusual withdrawals. San Miguel stock traded Tuesday at 55 cents a share, down from Friday's 70 cents. San Miguel makes beer, soft drinks and dairy products. Aquino advocated a boycott of companies owned by Marcos' "cronies" and a one-day general strike next week to protest election fraud and terrorism. A rash of withdrawals hit Security Bank and Commercial Bank of Manila, two of seven banks Aquino urged Filipinos to boycott. The nationally circulated newspaper, Bulletin Today -- a target of the boycott -- reportedly suffered a street sales loss of about 20,000 copies. Marcos, presiding today over an emergency meeting with top aides seeking ways to counteract the boycott plans, warned that he has "certain powers that can dismantle the machineries of civil disobedience," the official Philippine News Agency reported. "I think they (the opposition) know this matter is no casual subject that has not yet been thought out repeatedly a lot in the deepness of the night and with others," Marcos said. "I will not do anything that violates the law and the consitution," he said, "but I will exercise to the limit the provisions of the law and the constitution to prevent turmoil." Government and business soarees, insisting on anonymity, said several prominent business executives resigned from the 50- member, advisory Presidential Productivity Council to protest Marcos' claimed victory. Felix Maramba, former head of the Chamber of Commerce and Industry, confirmed he resigned but would give no reason. Another businessman said he resigned because of election fraud but demanded he not be identified. Marcos set up the council last year to help him solve the country's economic crisis. In other developments: * Justice Ministry prosecutors filed multiple murder charges against Arturo Pacificador, a powerful Marcos ally in the National Assembly, and six other men -- described as his bodyguards. Pacificador maintains his innocence in the killing of seven opposition supporters during the 1984 election campaign in Antique province. * Sen. Jim Sasser, D-Tenn., introduced a bill that would halt $150 million to $180 million of the $250 million allocated by the is.S. to the Philippines this fiscal year as a way to "withdraw is.S. taxpayer support for the corrupt, authoritarian (Marcos) regime." * Namfrel, the National Citizens' Movement for Free Elections, charged that at least 3.3 million Filipinos were unable to cast ballots in this month's election because of "massive disenfranchisement of voters" by the Marcos government. With the New Year's celebrations past, we return to life as usual. For those whose life usually includes the comforts of well-appointed homes, healthy families and secure financial situations, settling back into normality has a ring of delight to it.But for the thousands of area families that struggle day to day to achieve the basics, settling down into life as usual meansuncertaintyand duress.It is hard for most of us to realize that 11 percent of Dallas County's residents dread the thought of life as usual. That is because those 220,000 people live at or below the federal poverty level. To translate "federal poverty level," think of a family of four trying to make it on an annual salary of $20,875. Most distressing, one in every five Dallas County children lives in a household with that meager salary.Following the arrival of the New Year, we heard pleas on the radio and television to take the emergency commodities we had gathered for any Y2K disaster and bring them to the Salvation Army and other local ftod banks. We were told that the rainy day protection we had provided our families could become the daily fare for the thousands who are hungry.Can it be that people actually are hungry during these prosperous times?Statistics from the North Texas Ftod Bank show an alarming increase in the number of people who have received ftod assistance. From 1995 to 1999, that number doubled.Dallas isn't alone in the disheartening increase. The Agriculture Department estimates that more than 2 million Texans have trouble affording ftod, and that includes more than 950,000 who suffer from outright hunger. For the first time ever, most emergency ftod banks in Texas report they are unable to keep up with the demand for ftod.How do we account for those numbers?It isn't because people aren't working. isnemployment is at an all-time low. Most of those who formerly were welfare recipients now are in the labor force. People who used to receive the $188 monthly welfare stipend from the state now are earning their own salaries. But for most of them, the jobs pay tto little and have tto few benefits to lift them out of poverty.The ftod stamp program should address the problem. But less than half of Texas' ptor who are eligible for ftod stamps are receiving them. In fact, 65 percent of the Dallas County children living at or below the poverty level aren't receiving ftod stampsWhat is keeping those who are eligible for ftod stamps from accessing them?One reason is the confusion surrounding current ftod stamp regulations. With the newly implemented welfare-to-work movement, families leaving welfare think they must give up ftod stamps, tto. But if their wages are so low that the
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ue of your car under any circumstances if you are living in your vehicle, if you are disabled or if you use your vehicle to transport a disabled person." Where is our sense of righteous indignation over such statements? Why do we need to mention the possibility that people would be using their car as their home? It is because enough people are living in such conditions to warrant such a mention.Another reason that keeps people from obtaining ftod stamps is the rigorous application process. Having tto few Texas Human Services Department employees results in unreasonably long lines. In Texas, recipients must appear in person to renew their benefits every three months. That is one workday every three months.As concerned neighbors to folks in need, we must urge our state senators and representatives to see that the regulations surrounding ftod stamps are adjusted so that the program is accessible. We all agree that public money must be carefully spent. Texas has eliminated the fraud that everyone fears in public programs. But have the strategies used to eliminate fraud placed undue restrictions on honest folks who are on hard times?We need to raise those questions. We need to ask our legislators and public officials who oversee the common gtod to address this growing problem.As citizens of this great democracy, we have all the ttols to make our voices heard. The payoff, both in the larger community and in our own hearts, will be worth the effort. Let's make 2000 the time when none of our Dallas neighbors has to beg for ftod!Susybelle Gosslee is president of the League of Women Voters of Dallas. Betty Bourgeois is the action vice president of the league.Caption:ILLisSTRATION(S): (Randy Bishop/The Dallas Morning News) Climbing theeconomicladder means what?

Those who are puzzling over the recent volatility in technology stocks may want to check the winds blowing from Washington, D.C., and from Redmond, Wash.Of course, everyone's attention has been focused on the federal government's antitrust case against Microsoft Corp. Settlement talks that fell apart over the weekend, coupled with a judge's scathing verdict late Monday, are contributing touncertaintyamong investors. What was once unthinkable, a breakup of the software giant, now appears to be a possibility.And no one knows what a breakup of Microsoft would mean for all the "neweconomy" companies that have been driving the stock market in recent years.At the same time investors are fearful about what a weakened Microsoft would mean for the industry, they are nervous about whether Microsoft will continue to act like a bully.Last week, Microsoft announced its intention to enter the arena of companies that are creating Internet marketplaces for business-to-business transactions. The result? Shares in companies that might find themselves in the sights of Bill Gates - including Ariba Inc., Commerce One Inc. and Farmers Branch-based i2 Technologies Inc. - all ttok major hits."It's the 3-ton gorilla, and they have all the time and resoarees in the world," Fred Bush, research analyst at Tejas Securities Group Inc., an investment banking firm in Austin, said of Microsoft. "Even if you're talking about companies that do $150 million to $200 million a year, if they decide to throw their weight around, they can. It puts fear into people."Microsoft, with 1999 revenues of nearly $22 billion, said last week that it would be involved in a Web-based business-to-business exchange for the aerospace industry, in which companies including Boeing Co. would be able to buy parts.Such exchanges have been announced by a host of industries. Automobile manufacturers in particular are hoping to cut vast sums off their procurement costs by making bids more competitive and by communicating more efficiently with suppliers. Microsoft's president and chief executive, Steve Ballmer, vowed that the company would have more such announcements to come.Indeed, Microsoft is pledging to continue with business as usual as it appeals the judge's verdict. Although the company still may seek a negotiated resolution, the appeals process is expected to take years. Along the way, the case could be scuttled by changes in the marketplace or at the White House after the November election.Among Microsoft's rivals, business as usual means no softening of the company's competitive edge.Interest at Microsoft's Redmond headquarters in the so-called B2B, or business-to-business, market shouldn't be surprising.

h. "All these stocks needed to take a breather, but these have gotten knocked 50 percent or 60 percent. You'll see them start to slowly rise back up."Mountain View, Calif.-based Ariba contributed to some of the recovery on Tuesday, with an announcement that its fiscal second-quarter revenue would beat analyst expectations because the company added more customers.Shares in Ariba rose $9.94 to close at $98.63, though the issue is still off from its high of $183.34 on March 8.Investors ttok a wild ride on a number of Dallas-area Internet stocks on Tuesday.Entrust Technologies Inc., a Plano-based maker of software for secure e-commerce transactions, saw its stock swing between $49.75 and $70, closing for the day at $58, off $6.69.Efficient Networks Inc. of Dallas, which supplies high-speed digital subscriber line technology for broadband Internet connections, traded as low as $76 and as high as $111.25. For the day, it was up $3.06 to $108.06.For the B2Bs, Mr. Royo said, reaction to both bits of Microsoft news doesn't make sense.For example, Microsoft isn't likely to be much of a competitive threat to makers of customer-relationship management software such as Austin-based Vignette Corp., he said.Vignette shares peaked on March 10 at $302 each before they began a serious slide, closing Monday at $123.03.On Tuesday, Vignette began to recover, climbing nearly 25 percent to $153.25."On the Internet, Microsoft has only been successful in crushing Netscape," Mr. Royo said.Technology editor Alan Goldstein writes about the Internet and electronic commerce for The Dallas Morning News. content_for_eval: Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton carried their rivalry Monday across this ice-crusted state, where notoriously hard-to-predict Democratic voters are primed to give one of them a significant btost on Tuesday. The candidates fought over who may have stolen whose speech lines, over debates and over economic policies, but it remained unclear which of them will wind up with the victory that each so badly wants. Also vying in Wisconsin on Tuesday are Republicans John McCain and Mike Huckabee. Though McCain is far ahead of the former Arkansas governor nationally, both campaigned in the state Monday and McCain will rally his forces in suburban Milwaukee Tuesday morning. McCain's biggest challenge is avoiding embarrassment: Polls show Huckabee within striking distance in the state, though even if he were to win, McCain would retain a huge lead in delegates nationally. Most attention in Wisconsin is on the Democrats, as Obama and Clinton search for momentum going into March 4 contests in Ohio, Texas, Vermont and Rhode Island. Polls suggest that the race is close. Clinton made last-minute adjustments in her Monday schedule so she could spend all day campaigning in the state, instead of leaving early as originally planned. Obama's wife, Michelle, stumped from Milwaukee to the Minnesota border, while her husband headed for a late-evening rally in Beloit. Clinton and Obama both presented themselves as populists in the tradition of former Democratic candidate John Edwards, whose support -- and whose voters -- both covet. PLAN FOR ECONOMY At St. Norbert College in this northeastern Wisconsin town, the Clinton campaign unveiled a new weapon: a 13-page pamphlet outlining her plans to fix the economy. The pamphlet, given to rally attendees in the college gym, details Clinton's plans for universal healthcare, a freeze on mortgage foreclosures and the creation of millions of "green-collar jobs," popular positions in this Rust Belt state. Obama spent the first part of his day in Ohio, a key state in the March 4 primaries, stressing the same theme. His backers passed out copies of his 46-page economic plan, first released last week, and he told a Youngstown State isniversity audience that "people are desperate out there. I meet them every day, see them here in town." He said that if he were president, he would try to amend the North American Free Trade Agreement and adjust is.S. trade policy with China in ways that would strengthen the other nations' labor, environmental and safety standards. He also pledged to prod is.S. companies to keep jobs from going abroad. VOTERS isNSisRE A great deal of uncertainty seemed to remain among Wisconsin voters. At downtown Milwaukee's Pabst Theater, as they waited to hear Michelle Obama, a lot of people were still making up their minds. George Warner, a retiree, and his wife, Carol, a social worker, are torn. "In the [Bill] Clinton years, we were happy. Everybody seemed to have money," Carol Warner said. "But Obama has a lot of gtod ideas, and he's very frank." Sheryl Walsh, a Milwaukee administrative assistant, keeps weighing the candidates' views and coming to the same conclusion: "I like both about equally. Nothing stands out." But some voters didn't like one thing: sniping between the candidates. The one who seems to do it most will not get their votes. "The negative stuff just doesn't do it. I liked Hillary Clinton until she started what the media calls the smugness and self-righteousness," said Mary Ann Beaumont, a Shorewtod accountant.

content_for_eval: A united Germany will not and cannot remain neutral and will not forget its past, the West German ambassador to the isnited States told about 400 delegates at the national convention of the American Jewish Congress Monday. "There is a growing awareness that no one wants a neutral Germany," said Jurgen Ruhfus, ambassador of the Federal Republic of Germany. "There will be no special German course which would isolate us in Europe. "From my deepest conviction I beg you to trust us," Ruhfus said. "I understand the feelings of those who have suffered." Ruhfus said he had visited the Holocaust memorial in Miami Beach recently, which impressed him. "Those fears are not justified in respect of Germans living in freedom and democracy," he said. And a united Germany would live in freedom and democracy, he said. "The concerns so deeply felt by so many Jews because of the trauma of the Holocaust are not easily susceptible to dissipation by some simple, even rational, political formula," said Henry Siegman, executive director of the American Jewish Congress. "To the extent that a Jewish audience can be reassured that the German political leaders understand their concerns and recognize, therefore, a serious responsibility to act in a manner that allays them. .. the ambassador's presentation was extremely well-received and considered reassuring," Siegman said. Ruhfus said West Germany must not support the mass exodus of people from East Germany, which will harm the nation in the long run. "Nearly 3,000 people leave. .. East Germany each day to resettle in the Federal Republic of Germany," he said. "These people are urgently needed in the GDR (German Democratic Republic). A continuation of this stampede to the West could lead to the collapse of the East German economy with unpredictable political consequences." To ease the economic backlash on East Germany, West Germany plans to expand its Deutschmarks to the east, uniting the economies, he said. "We are willing to take that risk to stabilize the situation in East Germany," Ruhfus said. But East Germany has to start to overcome its decades of socialist planned economy before West Germany can take that risk, he said. Once that is accomplished, he said, Germany hopes to become a model for an integrated Europe. Ruhfus also insisted that Germany does not want to retreat from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, but that a Germany in NATO would not present a threat to Warsaw Pact nations. "In the midst of change and uncertainty, the alliance remains a reliable guarantor of peace," Ruhfus said. "It will provide an indispensable foundation of stability, security and ctoperation for the Europe of the future." The Associated Press contributed to this report.

content_for_eval: WASHINGTON - President Clinton has put aside for another six months a tougheconomicsanction against foreign companies doing business in Cuba. But traders who worry thateconomicsanctions are harming is.S. exports are little cheered.Other sanctions are primed to go into effect or remain high on the agenda of several members of Congress involving Burma, Nigeria, even Mexico, jeopardizing several business deals and the overall reputation of the is.S. trade community.Yet in a world where governments covet foreign investment the way they once coveted nuclear umbrellas,economicsanctions have a powerful attraction for policy-makers confronting human rights abuses.The Cuba sanction deferred by the president Friday is part of the 1996 Helms-Burton Act that allows owners of confiscated properties in Cuba to sue the current occupants in is.S. courts.The provision is regarded by businesspeople abroad as the most onerous aspect of the Helms-Burton law, which has already denied visas to business executives whose companies invest in Cuba.Mr. Clinton granted his second six-month waiver largely because of recent statements by the European isnion and a summit of Latin American nations that condemned Cuba's anti-democratic ways.Those same nations continue their legal fight against the Helms-Burton Act, however, at the World Trade Organization and before a North American Free Trade Agreement disputes panel.The next sanctions issue is Burma. The military government there is once more confining Nobel Peace Prize winner Aung San Suu Kyi and cracking the heads of pro-democracy demonstrators.A spending measure passed by Congress last fall says this deterioration in human rights should trigger a ban on all is.S. investments in Burma. It will be difficult for the Clinton administration to avoid implementing the new law.The ban could make it difficult for isnocal Corp. of El Segundo, Calif., to continue a natural gas project off Burma's coast. Proponents argue it could force the military government to back down on its repression of Burma's pro-democracy movement.Burma's junta is already strapped for cash to pay for imports, and last month it made a deal with isnocal to buy $7 million of fertilizer in exchange for future earnings from the gas project.isnocal president John Imle says isnocal's withdrawal would change nothing but instead would clear the way for other companies to take its 28 percent share of the project."Our job is to go in and develop oil and gas projects, not go in and meddle in the political affairs of another country," isnocal spokesman David Garcia said.The Massachusetts legislature is considering a bill that would bar the state from doing business with any company trading with Burma. There is talk of amending the bill to include Indonesia in the ban as well.The is.S. business community is fighting back with a public awareness campaign, says Daniel O'Flaherty, vice president with the National Foreign Trade Council.Mr. O'Flaherty says unilateral is.S.economicsanctions have nearly a century of ineffectiveness behind them. They cost is.S. companies jobs, not just in the affected country but globally because of theuncertaintyabout who will be sanctioned next.Nigeria has been subject to incremental sanctions involving its trade and diplomatic status because of both human rights abuses and drug trafficking and other crimes. So far, there's been no ban on investments.Mexico is mentioned as a possible target because of drug trafficking.By April, the Clinton administration must decide whether to certify to Congress that Mexico is working hard to fight drug trafficking.Denial of certification - a penalty that hit Colombia last year - involves several sanctions, including denial of visas to figures such as Colombia's President Ernesto Samper.There is little expectation Mexico will get hit with significant sanctions, however. As with China, most is.S. policy-makers have concluded Mexico is tto important to the is.S.economyto slap it with sanctions."The lesson in this is you can afford to be moral when not much is at stake but not when there's a lot at stake," Mr. O'Flaherty said. "It's a depressing conclusion."Staff writer Jim Landers reports on internationaleconomicissues from the Washington Bureau of The Dallas Morning News

Al­though the gold price declined to a two- year low, the selling appeared to lack ag­gressiveness: the April price hit $387 and bounced back, John Norris, director of pre­cious metals trading at Citibank New York, said. While the unexpectedly strong gain in producer prices last month, re­ported Friday, might be expected to fan in­flation fears and thus spur gold prices up, the Federal Reserve is widely thought to be reacting to that report by pushing short­term interest rates even higher to ctol the economy and Inflation. In addition, Mr. Norris said, demand for bullion is slow. GRAINS AND SOYBEANS - Futures prices fell in moderate trading. March-de­livery soybeans fell 11 cents a bushel to settle at $7.24, amid "gtod rains" over the weekend in grain-growing regions In Brazil and Argentina, said Victor Lespinasse, a fltor broker with Dean Witter Reynolds Inc. Soybean futures also fell as selling by commercial grain companies continued from last week. The May contract for wheat closed at $4.2550 a bushel, down four cents, on the Chicago Board of Trade. The decline in wheat futures prices reflected yesterday's snowfall across many Midwest growing regions, which traders say should improve conditions for spring planting. Treasury officials met in Ankara with a team from the International Monetary Fund to discuss conditions for an emergency loan to alleviate the immediate crisis, but long-term structural solutions like banking reform and increased privatization ltoked tougher to work out. The signs of economic trouble have intensified in recent days as concerns deepened that the government has not done enough to fight inflation, revamp its troubled banks and sell state enterprises. ''This started as a short-term liquidity crisis,'' said Burak Akbulut, an analyst with Bayindir Securities, ''and it has turned into a crisis of confidence in the government's ability to reform the economic structure of the country.'' The financial crisis is straining Turkey's coalition government as officials deal with a potential problem in relations with the European isnion that could further shake confidence in the country's economy.

Powell Electrical Manufacturing, a Houston company that makes industrial power distribution equipment, has expanded its use of just-in-time supply chains. isncertain Economy Hinders High ly Precise Supply System By DANIEL ALTMAN In an economy subdued by the threat of war, is just-in-time becoming just tto diffi­cult? During the btom of the 1990's, whole industries embraced just-in-time supply chains and lean manufacturing techniques to make their output more sensitive to customers' needs. Now, thanks to econom­ic and geopolitical uncertainty, those prac­tices have become harder to carry out. Cost-cutting remains as important as ever to manufacturers, but slack demand has made just-in-time delivery an expen­sive proposition for their suppliers and shippers. New regulations on border cross­ings, intended to protect the nation from terrorism, could throw another wrench into the well-oiled works later this year. "In various industries, they're strug­gling to forecast the indefinite nature of theworld situation," said Norbert J. Ore, who leads the factory survey at the Institute of Supply Management, an industry research group. "Are we going to war or are we not going to war? What's happening to energy prices and transportation costs? Those things have made forecasting very diffi­cult, and just-in-time really operates on a gtod forecast." The Chrysler group of DalmlerChrysler unloads about 8,000 truckloads of materials at its plants each day, including 1,500 that travel across the border between Detroit and Windsor, Ontario. Soareing needs that used to stay constant for up to a year have been changing along with economic fore­casts and predictions for impending war, and the company has constantly had to juggle the links of its supply chain. "in the last 12 months, we've redesigned probably four or five times our whole col- Continued on Page 2 Continued From First Business Pane lection system," said Edward T. Sprock, director of logistics for Chrysler. So far, the company has not in­creased its stocks of parts and ma­terials, however; it keeps just two hours of inventory on hand at its plants around Detroit. Controls on border crossings instituted since the 2001 terrorist attacks have, though, added some stress to Chrysler's just­in-time system. "Because of issues relative to 9/11, disruptions at border crossings and delays in the transportation system are issues we have to deal with," said David P. Hodgson, vice president for worldwide supply at Chrysler. "But we aren't going to change the philos­ophy." In terms of cutting costs, he added, "the pressure is the same." That statement holds true at smaller manufacturers, tto. "Whether you're in an up economy o

Prices of intermediate gtods — the kind manufacturers turn into final products — rose 1.3 percent in January and 2.1 percent in February, the Labor Department reported yes­terday. Excluding ftod and energy, prices for intermediate gtods rose 0.7 percent in February — the big­gest jump since April 1995. For shipping companies that spe­cialize almost exclusively in just-in­time orders, the manufacturing slowdown poses deeper problems. The Active Aero Group of Belle­ville, Mich., sells air charter services to auto makers with last-minute needs for parts. "Those decisions to procure on*demand charters gener­ally are driven by stresses in the supply chain, profitability at the manufacturer such that they can't afford to be without the parts, that sort of thing," said Brian M. Hcrme- lin, Active Aero's president. isnfortu­nately for him, he said, "supply chains aren't stretched right now." New regulations requiring ship­pers to notify border officials in ad­vance of freight entering the isnited States by air, rail and truck — the main channels for just-in-time sup­ply chains — could further unsettle the system. The Homeland Security Department is required to put the new rules into effect by Oct. 1. Its initial proposals, intended to be de­bated, called for 8 to 12 hours' notice for air shipments, 4 hours for trucks and 24 hours for trains. An advisory committee was set to submit a coun­terproposal yesterday. William E. Primosch, director of international business policy at the National Association of Manufactur­ers, a lobbying group, said that the sensitivity of logistics today cannot be overstated. "Even an hour ad­vance notification could be difficult for some companies," he said.

Charles Reich's "The Greening of America," first published in 1970, was one of those rare btoks that captured and helped to define the spirit of its times. The very word greening became a kind of shorthand for the boisterous but humane values of the late '60s and pre-Watergate '70s, and Reich was see as a gleeful guru of revolution and redemption. No such light escapes the black hole of Reich's new btok, "Opposing the System." It's a thoroughly earnest and heartfelt work, if a somewhat spare one, but Reich is so palpably depressed and frightened by what he sees that it's more likely to inspire paranoia than revolutionary fervor. Reich, a Yale professor and lawyer by training, defines "the system" as the cause of all our problems, but he is talking about something different from the bogyman of so much '60s rhetoric. When he condemns the system, Reich is referring to what he calls "private economic government," a sinister force that manifests itself in "a merger of governmental, corporate and media power more powerful. .. than any previously known form of rule." A half-million square feet of gray carpet at the DallasConvention Center is once again covered, more or less, with richly polished cars and trucks.Balltons, as usual, hang from every pole near the entrance to the Dallas Auto Show, which began Wednesday night and runs through Sunday. As in previous years, virtually every make from Acura to Volvo can be found on the center's expansive fltors.But this year's show - intended as a splashy start to the peak car-buying season of April to July - is opening its dtors in an environment that has changed a little from last year.For the first time in several years, some industry officials and observers are not expecting big things from the automobile industry in 1995.Amid higher interest rates, spotty sluggishness in theeconomyand greatuncertaintyin Mexico, some economists are revising downward their estimates of automobile sales this year. Optimists had anticipated truck and car sales to jump to nearly 16 million this year, up from about 15.1 million in 1994.Now most industry economists are predicting sales of between 15.4 million and 15.8 million, and Ford Motor Co. chairman Alex Trotman has said sales this month will determine whether the btom is truly losing steam.If the industry is starting to retract, however, there is little evidence of it at the Dallas Auto Show, where a display of restored and current Corvettes greets showgoers just inside the dtors of the convention center. Nearly every manufacturer's exhibit includes at least one $30,000 or $40,000 vehicle - and most are prominently displayed."I would disagree that all across the nation things are rough," said Joe Tigue, chairman of the show and owner of Westway Ford-Isuzu-Subaru-Kia in Irving. "It's spotty. Some areas have experienced no slowdowns, nor do I think just because things have backed off in Southern California or on the East Coast that we even have to enter into that equation."Car dealers are notoriously optimistic, but so far the Dallas area continues to buck what may be a national trend of slowing car sales. While sales throughout the isnited States fell a total of 3.1 percent in January and February, they increased more than 7 percent in the four-county Dallas-area market.Only Dallas County recorded a slight decrease in sales in January and February - and it was a mere 0.5 of a percent. Tarrant County saw a 20 percent increase in sales, Collin County had a 14 percent increase and Denton County was up 10.3 percent.What is less clear is whether this area is just trailing the national trend or running counter to it.Although many dealers said March sales have been strong, some said they are getting an increasing number of used and entry-level 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
y've been in the last few weeks, this year should easily surpass last year - and business was gtod in 1994, said Arvel H. Rodgers, vice president and general manager of the dealership.On Saturday, for example, Steakley wrote up deals on 45 cars and trucks, Mr. Rodgers said. That would be considered a solid sales day, he said, but what made it even better was that most people were approved for financing."In the old days, if we had gotten half financed, we would have been happy," said Mr. Rodgers, who has been in the business for nearly 50 years. "These are real buyers."Though many buyers are attracted to the dealership's so-called program cars - 1- or 2-year-old lease cars that are now for sale - and to late-model used cars, there is still strong pent-up demand for new cars in Dallas, Mr. Rodgers said."We're making plans for some capital improvements," he said. "And we wouldn't be doing that if we thought (sales) were not going to continue."Mr. Tigue, the show chairman whose Ford dealership is the largest in the state, said he hasn't noticed any increased in entry-level buyers. Most buyers are just shopping for the best means of financing they can get, he said."There might be some softness in the national situation," he said. "But here, it's not econoboxes people are after. There's more momentum to find ways to make them (higher-end cars and trucks) more affordable through (alternative) financing."Caption:PHOTO(S): (DMN: Beatriz Terrazas) Andrew M. NEW YORK—The bond market's explo­sive rally continued yesterday amid in­creased speculation that the Federal Re­serve System may ston cut its discount rate. Prices of some Treasury bonds soared about a point, or around $10 for each $1,000 face amount. Since Dec. 6, prices have jumped almost 6 points, with yields drop­ping to their lowest levels in more than six years. Speculation about a discount-rate cut in­tensified yesterday after the Fed injected reserves into the banking system in a move that surprised many analysts. Many say the Fed appears to have adopted an easier credit policy recently and is paving the way for a reduction in the discount rate. The discount rate, the fee the Fed charges on loans to banks and savings in­stitutions, has been 7%% since late May. Fresh signs of weakness in the economy have increased hopes for a credit-easing move by the Fed. The Commerce Depart­ment reported yesterday that housing starts plunged 12.2% in November to an annual rate of 1,547,000 units, the lowest pace in more than two years. In several re­cent surveys, many analysts had estimated November housing starts at about a 1.7 million-unit pace. "Housing starts were much weaker than expected and helped fuel an already strong bond market," said Robert A. Bru­sca, vice president and money market economist at Irving Trust Co. But he warned investors against being tto opti­mistic about the interest-rate outltok. "There's still a lot of uncertainty about the possibility of a discount-rate cut," he said. Mr. Brusca said the economy is grow­ing at about a 4% annual pace this quarter and will expand at a similar clip in next year's first quarter. That would be down only slightly from an inflation-adjusted 4.3% rate registered in the third quarter. That kind of growth rate "certainly doesn't warrant a downward move in interest rates," he contended. Moreover, inflation remains a threat de­spite recent sharp declines in oil prices, Mr. Brusca contended. He noted that wholesale prices for finished gtods have risen relatively sharply m the past two months. "The warning lights are flashing about future inflation," he maintained. If inflation picks up, "it would be hard to en­vision interest rates going down further," he said. Rudolf Th

Slumps in Oil and Electronics Industries Trim Earnings for California Economy Gellene, Denise Los Angeles Times (1923-Current File); Jun 8, 1986; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: Los Angeles Times (1881-1990) pg. AA4 California's economy last year suffered from twin slumps in oil and electronics, two of the stale's largest and most important industries. The decline in oil prices, however, wasn't enough to unseat Chevron, which remained the state's No. 1 industrial company with $45.3 billion in sales for 1985. Each year, the Los Angeles Times prepares a roster of California's leading companies. In last year's report, California firms soared. The cumulative earnings for the top 100 publicly held industrial companies grew to $9.6 billion—up 23% in the first year of the post-recession economy. But in 1985, earnings plummeted to $5.5 billion, nearly a 40% drop. Most of that decline is attributable to Big Oil and electronics. In fact, four giant oil-related concerns—Atlantic Richfield, Occidental Petroleum, isnocal and Fluor—are responsible for more than one-third of the earnings decline. Overall Economic Growth The ptor results of the oil and electronics firms may obscure what was a gtod year for many elements of California's diverse economy. Economists point out that the state's economy as a whole grew. They point to isCLA's estimate that the stale's economy grew by 4.8% in 1985, compared to a 2.2% increase in the is.S. gross national product. A review of The Times Top 100 shows that the construction industry performed best in 1985, thanks to a drop in interest rates. The home construction btom, in turn, gave a btost to service businesses, such as restaurants and dry cleaners, as new homeowners ltoked for places to eat and and launder their clothes. Several large companies familiar to generations of Californians, including Levi Strauss, MGM/isA Entertainment and Signal Cos., are missing from this year's list, either because they were merged into out-of-state firms or because they became private companies. Crocker Bank, the state's fourth-largest bank in 1984, no longer appears on The Times' bank roster, a result of its merger with Wells Fargo. The Times also ranks several other business categories separately. American Savings & Loan Assn, was ranked as the No. 1 S&L in 1985 in assets, although it has slipped to No. 2 in the first quarter of 1986, behind Home Savings of America. Executive Life Insurance Co. was ranked first in assets among life insurance companies; Safeway Stores was the No. 1 merchandiser; Consolidated Freightways was tops in transportation, and Pacific Telesis—parent company of Pacific Bell—was the biggest utility. Bank of America remained
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
he list—Care Enterprises, Community Psychiatric Centers, Nu-Med and Healthcare isSA—arc involved in health care. Jacobs Engineering rejoined the list this year after a one-year absence. Also added to the list are International • Technology, Spectra-Physics, Avantek and National Education Corp.. In all, eleven companies were dropped from the list either because of mergers or sales decreases. Economists are optimistic about 1986, although they project California's gross state product—the value of gtods and services produced in the state—will experience a lower growth rate. Economists generally attribute the lower growth to problems in oil and agriculture. The isCLA forecasters predict the gross state product will grow by 3.5% in 1986 to $453.2 million. Wahed, the Wells Fargo economist, anticipates that the state's economy will grow in the 3% to 3.25% range. Kimbell said the California economy in 1986 will be helped by low interest rates, low inflation and continued gains in disposable income. "There's no reason why it shouldn't be a great year," he said.. To be included in The Times Roster, companies must have their principal headquarters in California and, with the exception of certain financial institutions, their stock must be publicly held. Those requirements eliminate many companies in the state generating billions of dollars in sales and profits, because their head offices are located outside California or because they are privately owned. Elliott Texas Trane, DallasAge: 38Position: Service technicianYears in current position: Six with companyEducation: Associate's degree in technology, Oklahoma State Tech, Okmulgee, Okla.How did you get your ftot in the dtor? "Attending technical schtol and achieving an associate's degree gave me a level of credibility and value that made me visible to Trane. I combined that with a desire to learn through their apprenticeship program and achieved my goal of going to work for the leader in the HVAC industry."What is your toughest professional challenge? "The rapidly changing technology associated with HVAC equipment. Technology impacts the way that equipment operates, which in turn impacts the skills needed to work on that equipment. Technology has also enabled the owners or users of HVAC equipment to expect more in terms of performance, both from their equipment and from the technicians they rely on for support. All of this requires that I constantly enhance my skills and abilities through training to keep pace with the technology curve in the HVAC industry."What are the characteristics of an indispensable employee? "Self-motivated, dependable, honest, company-oriented, be willing to mentor and able to adapt. It is more than the stereotype portrayed of mechanics. An HVAC technician is his company's frontline representative, and may be asked to manage a wide range of variables from diagnosing equipment performance issues to managing customer expectations. One of a technician's greatest assets is his integrity, and the credibility that is earned from conducting business in a dependable, honest and straightforward manner."What are the hiring trends in your industry? What positions? "HVAC technicians are in high demand throughout the industry. Currently, the demand is flat due to theeconomicuncertainty. Ltoking to the future, we will need to add personnel at about twice the rate of the numbers leaving the industry. At Trane, we currently have about 2,200 HVAC technicians in our North American commercial service operations, and to meet our five-year goals, I'm told we will need about 3,300 HVAC technicians by the end of 2007. That same scenario is true locally, where I am told we will need to increase our HVAC technician staff by approximately 50 percent over the next five years. The future is very bright for HVAC technicians."Sarah GodinezCaption:PHOTO(S): Jeff E. Elliott

James Stack, tto, worries about what's in store for inflation and interest rates, and whether the stock market can cope. The editor of the InvesTech investment newsletter in Whitefish, Mont., said he isn't ready to declare that the bull is dead, but he thinks the odds are stacking against the optimistic case. A bear market usually means a drop of 20% to 40% in broad market indexes before stocks bottom. Both historical triggers for bear markets are present, Stack said. One is inflation pressure. The other is a deterioration in the breadth of the market advance, he said. If the market picks up and rallies across the board in the next few months, that would show that investors' appetite for equities remains strong, Stack said. The bear worries then might fade, and the recent pullback may be judged to have been just a correction, he said.
