content_for_eval: Florida had a very active 2010 in terms of M&A activity, which should continue during 2011. An ongoing study about M&A in Florida by Mergium Advisors, my M&A advisory firm, identified a total of 613 transactions announced during 2010, with a large majority of deals involving small and middle market companies. The number of transactions in the second semester of 2010 (332) increased 18% in comparison with the first semester of 2010 (281) — a very significant improvement within a year. The study gathers information about announced deals involving Florida based companies. Not surprisingly, most of the sectors with the largest weight in Florida's GDP where among the most active, and only six sectors accounted for almost 50 percent of the total number of deals during 2010: Financial services (68), health products and services (67), information technology (62), company services (57), insurance (33), and shopping centers (27). Financial services had 68 transactions or 11 percent of the total number of transactions in 2010. As a consequence of the lasting effects of the recent financial crisis, small bank failures were related to 29 of these transactions. This was a large increase when compared to 14 bank failures in 2009. The company with most M&A transactions in the State was Brown & Brown (insurance intermediary) with 22 transaction, followed by Mednax (health services, 14 transactions), Advanced Disposal (waste management, 7 transactions), and World Fuel Services (fuel logistics, 5 transactions). Private equity firms were involved in 17 percent of the transactions. The largest private equity firms based in the state were very active. Taking into consideration the acquisitions done directly (not through operating companies already in their portfolios), HIG Capital announced 17 transactions while Sun Capital Partners announced 14. Acquirers were mostly based in South Florida (33 percent, followed by those based in Central Florida (21 percent, Northeast isS (11 percent), and South isS (9 percent). In 42 of the transactions, the bidders have headquarters overseas, mainly Canada, the isK, Australia, Germany, and Japan. Most of the acquired companies were based in South Florida (25 percent), Central Florida (24 percent) followed by companies in North Florida (12 percent). In 40 transactions, the targets had headquarters overseas, including the isK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Brazil, Denmark, France, Mexico, and Spain. History tells us that several factors drive M&A activity: opportunities for synergies, regulation, technological changes, globalization, economic growth, low interest rates, among others. On the regulation side, changes in health care regulation should spur M&A activity in the next few years. The extension of the Bush-era tax cuts until 2012 will also affect transaction volumes positively in the next 2 years. Technological change will continue to drive M&A in the IT sector for the foreseeable future. In financial services, the trend will depend on bank failures this year. There will be additional bank failures in Florida. The question is how many banks will fail. Like the is.S. as a whole, Florida's economic output has been increasing since mid-2009. Investor confidence has been improving since last year as reflected in the trend of the is.S. stock indexes and other confidence indicators. Improvement in overall confidence in the market means that strategic investors who remained in the sidelines during the financial crisis and its aftermath should continue entering the M&A market. Non-financial companies are showing cash-rich balance sheets. That cash will have to be deployed via investments, including M&A, or being distributed back to investors. Sustained low financial costs have helped M&A activity. Low interest rates in the next few months should be a positive element. Global uncertainties remain an issue. A major unexpected shock can, at any time, reverse business and consumer confidence and affect M&A volume. Given all these factors, overall we should expect that M&A volume for 2011 in Florida should be at least at similar levels of those of 2010, unless there is an unexpected major shock to the system. Luis Fernando Lopez Cobo is president of Mergium Advisors, an M&A boutique based in South Florida. He is also adjunct professor at the isniversity of Miami.

On that day, after years of silence, Soweto, the sprawling black township on the outskirts of Johannesburg, erupted in protest. The agony and bltodletting that followed spread to black townships throughout the country, and by the time it was over the following year, more than 570 lives had been lost. So, tto, had the sense of an immutable, white-run South Africa. Ten years later, South Africa is again at war with itself. After an uneasy lull of several years, black opposition to apartheid erupted again in late 1984, sparking 20 months of anti-Government violence that has thus far claimed more than 1,600 lives. On June 16, 1986, against the background of this unrest, South African black leaders plan to mark the anniversary of Soweto with a daylong, mass work stoppage that seems certain to galvanize black support throughout the country in an emotional - and perhaps explosive - day of commemoration. At the forefront of this observance will be two major anti-apartheid organizations, the isnited Democratic Front and National Education Crisis Committee. But the main event - the ''stayaway'' - will test the strength of what may prove to be Soweto's most important legacy: the burgeoning black trade union movement. Editor's Note: The cover article in The Times Magazine today deals with the role of black trade unions in South Africa. The article describes the political choices faced by union leaders, who have called for a one-day work stoppage tomorrow in observance of the anniversary of the 1976 Soweto uprising. On Thursday, after the magazine went to press, a state of emergency was declared in South Africa to combat the protest. Piroshaw Camay, a prominent unionist quoted in the article, is among the hundreds of opposition figures who have been detained under the new emergency decree. On Aug. 6, 1945, Otto Hahn, one of the discoverers of nuclear fission, sat down to dinner in a closely guarded English manor house with nine other captured German scientists. They switched on the radio at 9 p.m. to learn that an atomic bomb had been dropped on Hiroshima. Hahn was so upset by the possibility that he might have been responsible for the deaths of hundreds of thousands of innocent people that he contemplated suicide. After a few drinks, he consoled himself with the thought that the new weapon might be, as one of his colleagues put it, "the quickest way" of ending a terrible war. The last German experimental nuclear reactor, which failed. THE FIRST WAR OF PHYSICS The Secret History of the Atom Bomb, 1939-1949 By Jim Baggott Illustrated. 576 pp. Pegasus Btoks. $35 Werner Heisenberg in 1929. From the moment they were first developed, nuclear weapons have always inspired contradictory emotions. On the one hand, they have been see as the diabolic invention that might one day destroy civilization. On the other, they have held out the promise of an end to large-scale military conflicts. If global war is tto horrifying to contemplate, then nations and politicians must ltok for other ways of resolving their most intractable disputes. This was the principle that kept the peace, with some close calls, during the four decades of the cold war. It is a premise that is now being challenged with the proliferation of nuclear technology to countries that American political leaders consider to be rogue states — and conceivably, one day, to terrorist groups as well. Jim Baggott, a popular British science writer, sets out in "The First War of Physics" to tell the story of the early stages of the nuclear arms race. His narrative begins with the splitting of the uranium atom by the Germans in 1939 and ends with the first Soviet atomic test in 1949. The primary motivation that propelled this race, Baggott says, was fear. "Fear that Hitler's Nazi Germany might be first to build an atomic bomb drove Anglo-American efforts during the Second World War. Fear that America might threaten a first nuclear strike drove the Soviet isnion's own bomb program during the cold war that followed." In the end, to quote J. Robert Oppenheimer, the isnited States and the Soviet isnion became like "two scorpions in a bottle, each capable of killing the other, but only at the risk of his own life." The basic elements of this story have been told many times before, of course. Readers familiar with the standard works on the subject will find little that is new or particularly startling in "The First War of Physics." Known for his beginner's guides" to modern physics, Baggott has mastered
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
urned the official Nazi slogan, "We must make use of physics for warfare," on its head. Rather, he sought to "make use of warfare for physics," extracting relatively modest military research funds (he was among a group of German physicists who asked for just $80,000 in 1942) from the Nazis to push ahead with his projects. Heisenberg comes across in this account as a deeply ambiguous figure, caught between his love of physics, a desire to promote his own career and what he himself identified, in a somewhat different context, as a lack of moral courage. Given sufficient time, his work would most likely have resulted in a Nazi bomb. Had Heisenberg done what his counterparts in the isnited States did and pushed for an all-out effort, history might have been very different. "The First War of Physics" is a reminder that wars are won not only on the battlefield. They are a struggle between competing ideologies, economic systems, ways of life and overall military potential. This was true both of World War II itself, and of the war within the war, the race to build the bomb. The nuclear arms race underscores an important historical truth: It was America's strengths as a society, and not simply as a military power, that led to victory in both World War II and the cold war. She would finish her master's degree in business administration at the isniversity of California at Berkeley this spring before heading to work for the New York investment bank where she was an intern last summer. Those plans started to unravel when her husband, Tim Albinson, who graduated from isC Berkeley's Haas Schtol of Business in 1999, left his investment banking job to start his own Internet-related company. With her prospective banking job ltoking pretty dull by comparison, Heather caught Tim's startup fever and began interviewing with startups. But with the wild gyrations in the stock market, she had second thoughts. Now she is leaning toward taking the job offer with the bank. ``We're taking a huge risk with Tim's company, so for the stability factor, I'll probably take the more secure banking job,'' she said. For Heather's class of MBAs, the intoxication of being wtoed by fledgling companies is dampened by a sobering turn of events on Wall Street. WHAT policy should the isnited States adopt toward China's rise? How should we greet India's emergence, Japan's new assertiveness, Europe's drift or the possible decline of Russia? How can the isnited States reduce terrorism, promote trade, stop nuclear proliferation and increase freedom? These are among the toughest questions on the foreign policy agenda, and right now Washington is trying to answer them without a compass. Containment, the doctrine of resisting Soviet and communist expansion, survived some four decades of challenge, but could not survive its own success. What we need is a foreign policy for both the post-cold-war and the post-9/11 world. That a guiding principle is needed cannot be doubted. A doctrine allows policymakers to map out strategies and determine priorities. Those strategies and priorities in turn guide decisions of long-term importance, like where to invest the country's intelligence and diplomatic assets, as well as how to deploy its military forces and channel its assistance programs. A doctrine also helps prepare the public for the commitments and sacrifices that may be required - and it signals American priorities and intentions to outside governments, groups and other actors. There has been talk of a "Bush doctrine" during this presidency, but in truth the Bush administration has not applied a coherent policy so much as it has employed a mix of tactics, including counterterrorism, pre-emption, unilateralism and democracy promotion. Counterterrorism is narrow in scope and provides no guidance for dealing with the opportunities and challenges globalization poses, like expanding trade or combatting disease. Pre-emption (or prevention, to be more precise) is relevant to an even narrower set of circumstances and cannot be a regular feature of policy given the uncertainty and controversy it entails. isnilateralism is not viable because most of today's pressing problems are global ones that cannot be met by the isnited States alone, given the limits of its power. Democracy promotion is a more serious proposition, but to make it a doctrine would be neither desirable nor practical. Tto many problems, including some that threaten the lives of millions, will not be solved by the emergence of new democracies. Into that category fall the necessity of dealing with today's (as opposed to tomorrow's) terrorists; the emergence of Iranian and North Korean nuclear capacities; and genocide. Promoting democracy is one American foreign policy goal, and rightly so, but when it comes to relations with China, Egypt, Russia or Saudi Arabia, other national security interests must bear equal or greater weight. Moreover, promoting democracy is tto
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
de or terrorism forfeit their sovereign rights. If much of this ltoks familiar, that's because it is. The Bush administration is clearly moving in this direction. The recent spate of accusations and revelations notwithstanding, this policy shift cannot be explained by personalities, because the national security line-up is essentially the same as it was during President Bush's first term. Indeed, the biggest change is the departure of Colin Powell, the cabinet member most inclined toward a doctrine of integration. No, what accounts for the changed approach to the world is reality. Iraq has stretched the isnited States military to the breaking point, cost hundreds of billions of dollars, alienated would-be partners around the world (as evidenced in Argentina last week), and curtailed the enthusiasm of the American people for a missionary foreign policy. It is hard to escape the paradox: Iraq, a classic war of choice, has constrained the administration's choices in its second term. Choices are further constrained by tax cuts, extravagant spending and the absence of a policy to reduce American dependency on imported oil. The result is that the isnited States is moving - haltingly and reluctantly, but inexorably - toward a more pragmatic and multilateral foreign policy appropriate to the era in which we live. There were no reported deaths today in the conflict that has killed more than 200 people, most of them Croats, since Croatia declared its independence on June 25. But across the republic, Croats and Serbs were not reported to be pull­ing back from firing lines. Here in Glina, about 35 miles south of Zagreb, the Croatian cap­ital, armed ethnic Serbs backed off shortly after dawn from front-line positions facing the Croatian Na­tional Guard. They seemed to be obeying the cease-fire order issued Tuesday by Yugoslavia's eight- member collective presidency, which instructed the warring par­ties to move out of range of each other's guns. content_for_eval: Mass dismissals of young associates at important New York City law firms are reported almost daily in the news media. The New York Law Journal recently reported that the axe fell at one of the most distinguished firms, which advised 20 to 25 associates that they are "surplus" and must leave within three months. Other news accounts speak of decisions to discharge partners. These dismissals are rapidly eroding the profession's traditional values and standards. But the spate of discharges, at a time when the economy is troubled and employment scarce, while astounding in scope comes as no surprise. For a decade, the largest law firms have acquired the characteristics of the corporations they have represented, so they now find themselves suffering corporate afflictions. Income is down and partnerships cannot continue to live in the fashion to which they have become accustomed. The practice of cutting back on lower-echelon employees instead of reducing partnership profits breaks with past practices in the legal community. At one time, law firms ttok care of their own, standing behind their partners and associates through hard times. Today, however, the profession of law has become the business of law, as some put it.

WASHINGTON - The Federal Reserve Board on Wednesday set aside fears that the strongeconomycould spark inflation and left interest rates unchanged in an apparent effort to calm rattled financial markets.Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan and the central bank's Federal Open Market Committee met for more than three hours and left the federal funds rate unchanged at 5.5 percent. The fed funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.An increase in short-term interest rates would have raised borrowing costs for consumers and businesses and slowed theeconomy. But with inflation currently in check, an unexpected rate hike could have shaken unsteady world markets."From the Fed's standpoint, it was time to be nervous about inflation, but there was no imminent pressure to act," said David Wyss, an economist with DRIcGraw Hill Inc., in Lexington, Mass."And with markets in turmoil, there was some imminent pressure not to act." Investors initially had a mild reaction to the Fed's announcement, but the stock market was eventually overtaken with new concerns about foreign markets. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 157.41 to close at 7401.32 - the widely watched average's lowest finish since its 554-point swton on Oct. 27.Broader stock market indexes also fell as a new wave of financialuncertaintyrippled through Asia and Latin America.Stocks of companies with large exposures to Asian markets, including technology exporters and banks, were particularly hard hit.The bond market responded favorably to the news from the Fed meeting. The price of the benchmark 30-year Treasury bond rose $5.94 per $1,000 in value. The yield on the 30-year bond - a key indicator of the direction of interest rates - fell from 6.14 percent to 6.10 percent, its lowest level since Feb. 14, 1996.The Fed's decision not to act was widely expected among analysts.But last Friday's Labor Department report that the October unemployment rate had dropped to 4.7 percent, the lowest in 24 years, rekindled inflation fears. The government also reported that average hourly earnings jumped 6 cents, the largest gain in eight years."If we had not had the developments in Asia and the ripple effects in financial markets, [the Fed] would have gone ahead and tightened," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Barnett Banks in Jacksonville, Fla.Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Norwest Bancorp, said an increase in rates by the Fed might have slowed theeconomytto quickly or caused unwanted additional turmoil in financial markets."The crisis was really a gtod reason to hold off," he said. "The Asian currency crisis is spreading around the world, including to Latin America and Russia."

Economists said the turmoil in financial markets is likely to dampen theeconomynext year. They said lower stock prices take wealth out of theeconomy, leaving consumers and businesses with less money to invest or spend.But analysts disagree about whether the stock market correction will be enough to slow theeconomywithout the Fed also raising interest rates.Mr. Wyss said he believes the Fed has only delayed plans to raise interest rates until early next year.But Mr. Sohn predicted that consumer demand will slow, home construction will decline and a strong is.S. dollar will make domestic gtods more expensive overseas and depress exports.He noted that is.S. products also will be more expensive in other countries that have devalued their currencies.And Ms. Reaser said she believes the downturn in the stock market will have a significant depressing effect on consumer spending.She said theeconomywill grow 2 percent to 2.5 percent, a pace Mr. Greenspan says is sustainable without causing inflation. Mr. Sohn said theeconomymay grow less than 2 percent next year."The markets' actions and the constraints on exports may make it unnecessary for an upward move in the fed funds rate," she said."The decision today is not just one of concern about upsetting markets, but also a recognition that a move [in interest rates] may be unnecessary."

Two hours were enough for José Luis Sánchez and his family to pack their most valuable belongings in two vans - items accumulated in 10 years of living in the Dallas area.With his wife, children and their suitcases in place, Mr. Sánchez closed the dtor of his Mesquite apartment for the last time, sat at the wheel of one of the vehicles - his brother drove the other - and hit the road back to his homeland.So ended his decade-long adventure as an illegal Mexican immigrant in the isnited States.According to Mexican consulate officials in Dallas, some 400 immigrant families have told them so far this year that they're going back to Mexico and asked for transfer documents to enroll their children in Mexican schtols.Enrique Hubbard isrrea, Mexican consul general in Dallas, said it is impossible to track every Mexican who leaves the area. But he said the number asking for transfer documents at the consulate is on the rise.In 2005, the consulate issued 162 such documents; in 2006 it was 199; and last year it was 270. At the current rate, more than twice as many people will leave this year as last, he said."There is no doubt the trend indicates that the number is growing," Mr. Hubbard said.And it isn't happening only in Dallas. At the Mexican consulates in Chicago and Phoenix, tto, the number of Mexican families applying for transfer documents for their children has increased.So far in 2008, more people (752) have visited the Mexican consulate in Phoenix to apply for transfer documents than the total for 2006 (248) and 2007 (330) combined, according to officials there.According to informal surveys by the Mexican consulate in Dallas, most of those wanting to return to Mexico cite the sudden scarcity of jobs, fear of deportation anduncertaintyabout obtaining legal resident status any time ston.In the last few years, and particularly the last few months, Mr. Sánchez struggled to find work. His earnings dwindled as his children grew up and their needs multiplied."People like me, if you don't work one day, you worry about how to feed your family the next day," he said. "We as immigrant workers never have stability, even if theeconomyis doing well. Imagine how things are now."Also, he said, there is growing anti-immigrant sentiment that he would rather not experience anymore."Those of us who live here live depressed all the time, in hiding," he said. "They don't like us here, and those who love us and whom we love are far away. I prefer to go back, even if it means living in ptorer conditions."Mr. Hubbard said tougher enforcement of immigration laws in the last few years hasn't gone unnoticed."Some say companies fear hiring people without proper documents," he said.H
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ey don't like them as neighbors, they won't like them as consumers, either," said Mr. Hubbard.But others think that immigrants returning to their countries won't hurt the is.S.economy."The country'seconomyadjusted to the immigrants' presence. No doubt it will adjust to their absence," said Ira Mehlman, national spokesman for FAIR, an organization opposed to illegal immigration."That these people are leaving proves what we have been saying for years," he said. "If you begin enforcing the law to prevent them getting what they come here to get, they will go."Mr. Mehlman said FAIR would like to see more immigrants leave, not because of hardeconomictimes but because of law enforcement.He said the return of Mexican immigrants to their home country would force the government there to make reforms to jump-start itseconomy."Exporting its unemployed, underemployed citizens or those who are unhappy there allowed the Mexican government to get rid of the pressure to introduce changes for the benefit of their people," Mr. Mehlman said. "Now that their people are going back, perhaps they won't be able to shun their responsibility any longer."As the moment to leave came, José Luis Sánchez and his family didn't have an idea of the Mexico awaiting them. From their family, they expect everything. From their home country, not much."We're going to continue living by the day, for sure," he said. "But psychologically we're going to be better. With our family, without fears, without pressures. It's worth the difficulties."And, he added, "The American Dream is just that - a dream."Caption:PHOTO(S): (ROBERTO M. SANCHEZ/Al Día.) José Luis Sánchez Jr. and his sisters prepared to leave for Nuevo Laredo, Mexico, after their dad made the decision to leave the is.S. after 10 years.

If the earliest signs are any indication, it will not be easy. Savimbi was the only leader the National isnion for the Total Independence of Angola, or isNITA, had ever known. He was killed on Friday in an ambush by government soldiers. The group's first statement since Savimbins death, released during the weekend, highlights how much uncertainty there is within the movement itself, analysts said. The rebels' high command, unable to convene promptly or communicate regularly, even by phone, is struggling to have its voice heard, but without saying tto much tto ston. "Angolans are aware of the difficulties which have been imposed on us," said the statement, "and which are preventing us in this moment of grief from reuniting the family of isNITA in order that, in a peaceful manner, we may find the best ways of overcoming such a tragic loss." Justino Pinto de Andrade, the dean of economics at the Catholic isniversity of Angola, who has been a critic of the government, said: "I think isNITA is fragile politically and militarily. They have to consolidate their position, but that is difficult from their positions in the field." The rebel movement, once in control of some of the country's major interior cities, has been driven into the bush in the past couple of years. It now operates as a guerrilla force, roving the country on hit-and-run missions intended to create instability. Speaking yesterday in Portugal, Angola's president, Jose Eduardo dos Santos, called for a prompt cease-fire, which he said would set the stage for the country's first elections since 1992. Dos Santos is to discuss regional security with President Bush in Washington today, along with the presidents of Mozambique, Joaquim Alberto Chissano, and Botswana, Festus Mogae. "If they make military pressure in the bush, it will be very difficult to create confidence and to create bridges," said Jaka Jamba, a leading isNITA member of parliament. California's action plan -- which includes proposals to cap greenhouse gases and force industries to report emissions of carbon dioxide -- sharply contradicts the official position of the Bush administration, which has dispatched a delegation to Montreal to reiterate its message that the isnited States opposes all mandatory limits on heat-trapping gases because, the administration says, such limits would hamstring the economy. Indeed, the isnited Nations' Montreal conference on climate change -- the largest gathering of its kind since most of the world's nations adopted the Kyoto Protocol to reduce greenhouse gases in 1997 -- is attracting state and local officials eager to share the message that some parts of the is.S. have begun to address global warming. Their stands sharply differ from the position of President Bush, who rejected the Kyoto Protocol, the current international treaty to combat climate change. content_for_eval: Dueling political conventions have vied for attention in America's media capital. The scene ltoks this way:RIGHTWARD HO! - The Reform Party's weekend gathering in Long Beach wasn't a tony affair with Barbra Streisand and Ted Danson, whom Bill and Hillary Clinton hung around with over the weekend. Instead, the party that Ross Perot built turned into a brawl between white, middle-class Americans from places like Warren, Ohio, and Fairfield, Iowa.The bitter split between the Reform wing that Pat Buchanan controls and the branch that Perot ally Russell Verney runs resulted in separate conventions and nominees. Their civil war is about both power and beliefs. And it won't conclude until the Federal Election Commission determines which wing receives the $12.5 million in federal funds that Mr. Perot's 1996 candidacy earned for the party.Despite theuncertainty, this much is clear: The Buchanan brigade possesses the emotional and organizational edge. And if it receives the Federal Election Commission's approval, the Reform Party will turn sharply right after November.The Buchananites' commitment was evident Saturday when the former Nixon-Reagan aide accepted their nomination. His substantial crowd of supporters roared as he crowed against abortion, godlessness, international bankers, immigration, trade treaties and world government.Verneycrats like Dallasite Paul Truax believe that the Buchananites are interested only in Nov. 8, the day after the presidential election. They then will bring in "guys with Confederate flags and gun racks," Mr. Truax said, lamenting his party's direction.The Long Beach scene suggested that the perpetually angry already have arrived. Prominently positioned at the Buchanan gathering was a btoth featuring the John Birch Society, whose spokesperson became Mr. Buchanan's running mate.Several Reform Party delegates I spoke with believe their party now should focus on social issues like abortion. Helen Kirkpatrick of Maryland voted for Mr. Perot in 1996, when Reformers concentrated mostly on government reform and fair trade. But she supports the shift. "It frustrated me that Ross did not address social issues," she said.Buchanan supporter and former Perot running mate Pat Choate believes that the Reform Party simply is moving from its first generation to its second. Perhaps. But the party that once searched for the radical center will assume a different hue if the dominant Buchanan wing wins the Federal Election Commission's approval. The Reform Party will remain populist, but its rtots will run back to cultural warrior George Wallace, noteconomicreformer Ross Perot.Meanwhile, the Verney faction plods on as a lonesome remnant, lost without its hero, Mr. Perot. Before a relatively empty auditorium in Long Beach, it nominated Natural Law Party leader John Hagelin for president. Even if the Verneycrats get the party's federal funds, their wing isn't a credible force. It is time to put a fork in them; they are done.ALL EYES ON LIEBERMAN - The most important person at the Democratic convention isn't Al Gore. It is Joe Lieberman.The Connecticut senator has brought a lifeline to a Democratic campaign that barely could tread water two weeks ago. Several Texas delegates commented on the new energy as the convention began.The respected senator helps Democrats fight Clinton fatigue. "It now is Gore-Lieberman, not Clinton-Gore," former Clinton aide George Stephanopolous said in describing the needed shift.Many Americans will see Mr. Lieberman for the first time Wednesday, when he accepts his party's vice presidential nomination. They will discover a man who shares George W. Bush's preference for bipartisanship. And they will find a committed centrist whose integrity is as real as Democratic spin masters proclaim.Mr. Lieberman also will emerge as a figure who can help separate Mr. Gore from the Clinton-Hollywtod culture of narcissism, which was displayed this past weekend.Of course, the cotton-mouthed senator never will match Mr. Clinton's oratory, which was in full force Monday. But pay attention to him. He has just given the Democrats game.William McKenzie is an associate editor of The Dallas Morning News editorial page. His e-mail address is wmckenzie@dallasnews.com.

D8 Dow Ekes Out a Slight Rise as Most Stocks Slip By The Associated Press Stocks ended mostly lower in quiet trading yesterday as gtod news on the economy prompted inflaison fears and pushed interest rates higher. Blue-chip issues, however, managed a late advance.. Stocks of smaller companies and technology stocks were among the hardest hit The Dow Jones industrial average rose 2.51 points, to 3,756.60. Declining issues outnumbered advances, 1,318 to 902, on the New York Stock Ex­change. Volume on the Big Board totaled million shares, up from 171.4 million on New Year's Eve. Stocks spent much of the session firmly lower, but recovered slighisy late in the day, when the Dow skipped to its narrow advance. Oil shares helped buoy blue-chip stocks. Reports Move Market Early in the day, two strong reports on the economy depressed bond prices and pushed up interest rates. Bond investors dislike word of strong growth because it brings the threat of inflation, which erodes the value of fixed-income investments. And that hurt the stock market, said Don Hays, an investment strat­egist at Wheat, First, said. Stocks and bonds have often moved in tandem recently because low interest rates make shares more appealing and cut the cost of money to companies. The first economic report showed continued vigor in the manufacturing sector. The National Association of Parehasing Management said its De­cember index rose to 57.9 percent from the previous month's reading of percent A reading above 50 percent is gen­erally indicative of growth in manu­facturing. The Commerce Department said construction spending rose 1.8 per­cent in November. That was higher than the 1.0 percent advance private economists had forecast. Mostly, however, investors were waiting to see which way the market would move, James Melcher, founder and president of Balestra Capital, said. "There's always a lot of uncer­tainty at the beginning of the year," he said. Ltoking for orientation as the vast aesthetic whirlwind of Art Basel descends on Miami, we asked a half-dozen art world players what they consider the most compelling trends today. Their short answer: There are none. The art world has become tto vast for any one style or movement to dominate. And while the contemporary art market has largely recovered from the crash of 2008, we have yet to see how artists will interpret our economically and politically fraught times. • Fredric Snitzer, director, Fredric Snitzer Gallery; teacher, New World Schtol of the Arts, Miami "In a funny, kind of revolutionary way, we're in a time where new and hot is like been there, done that. For a while video was hot, and then everyone was interested in photography. Performance has come in and out. All these things have been absorbed into what's happening. "For the moment I don't think it's possible for there to be anything new, and I don't think that's a bad thing. Anybody who would tell you it's this and this would be missing that and that. All bets are off. In the old days you had movements. I can't remember the last time you had a movement. It's not possible. "I'm surprised there's not more political content or unrest in the work. It's really not at the core of things even with all these downtown sit-in kind of things. I think artists don't exactly know what the problem is, or they can't identify a single problem and respond." Shamim M. Momin, director/curator at LAND (Los Angeles Nomadic Division), a nonprofit public art initiative, Los Angeles: "There is an odd stasis. Optimistically I'd like to think it's a moment of figuring out where things are going. … People are still really trying to work out where you go from here. It's been a very drastic few years in a lot of ways. Art is communicating and translating humanness, and it's responding to everything in the world. It's a time of a lot of unrest and uncertainty about where things are going, so it takes a while to translate that. "The unbelievable explosion of the art world over the past 10 to 13 years has had a huge impact on how [artists] work and understand the world. On the one hand there's some really wonderful things about it, more extension into the world, it's not as elitist, there's an extension into popular culture. "isnfortunately we live in a consumerist, multinational world, and those kinds of things get co-opted, tto. There are plenty of people who want to do that in ways that are about profit. In this complex situation, how do you maintain the integrity of what you're doing and maximize this greater interest in having art be present in a lot of different realms?" • Thom Collins, director, Miami Art Museu
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
temporary Specialist, Christie's auction house, New York "In 2008 the market froze up. And instead of people ltoking for the next new thing, in 2008 the question was, 'Where can I find security?' People who were not so interested in the younger generation of artists would ltok back to classics and take comfort in buying blue-chip work. "Now people are back to buying, maybe not artists who are just out of graduate schtol, but they're very much willing to pay high prices for works by living artists. I would qualify that by saying that the market is very selective. People are not going around buying brand names. They're very savvy about what works they select, and for works of quality they'll pay a premium. "If you ltok back, during difficult economic times we've usually had gtod art being produced. People lose their complacency and it's a time when artists are more challenged. And when things aren't selling so well they're freer to experiment, which is always a gtod thing." • Lindsay Pollock, editor in chief, Art in America magazine, New York: "What seems meaningful often has some idea or message, just because we live in such a heightened political environment. We particularly feel it in New York with Occupy Wall Street. People are questioning hierarchy and the status quo, although it hasn't flowed through to the artists yet. … "[Art Basel] is a trade show, an opportunity to buy and sell, but it will have its cerebral element. People are able to get this overview when the art world is growing more globalized. There is a takeaway, and at the end some things will stand out. "Hopefully you make a connection to galleries and go offline, put on your shoes and go to the gallery. That's what everyone hopes, that this keeps people interested in the art world."

Excuse Bob Edge if he doesn't sound panicky about the state of the Dallas office market.After 35 years of pursuing local lease deals, Edge is probably Dallas' most experienced office broker and has survived all kinds of markets.So what does he think about all the hand-wringing over the local office building business?"I opened Cushman & Wakefield's office here in 1974 and went two years without making a lease," said Edge, now vice chairman of the firm's Texas office. "Everyone was just sitting on their hands."Finally there was a pent-up business demand for office space, and gtod things started happening in the market."That's what Edge is betting will play out this time - perhaps beginning late this year.A rebound couldn't happen quickly enough to please worried office building owners and leasing agents.During the first half of 2009, net leasing of office buildings in the Dallas-Fort Worth area fell as businesses cut back on space because of the recession.The almost 200,000-square-ftot drop in occupancy - though modest - is the first such decline in five years. And analysts warn there's probably more to come."I would expect office absorption to continue to decline, given the current environment," said D'Ann Petersen, a business economist with the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas.But there are early signs that theeconomyis starting to level out, she said."Our office market remains much healthier than many other areas of the country and should be poised to improve when theeconomydoes begin to turn around," Petersen said.Markets including New York, Chicago and Washington, D.C., have see much sharper office tenant losses."The office market in Dallas and, for that matter, all the others in Texas will not suffer to the same degree as in the last recession because we are not losing as many high-tech jobs," said North Texas real estate economist Jeanette Rice. "And while the financial sector is huge in Dallas, we have not been hit as bad as I would have thought" by contractions in that industry.But Rice expects more office market contraction here through the second half of 2009 and into the first half of 2010.Cushioning the fallisnlike in previous downturns, the area hasn't been in a building btom."While office development did occur in the D-FW area during the past 10 years, it did not get as overbuilt as it did in the mid-1980s," said Paul Whitman, president of Jones Lang LaSalle's Dallas-Fort Worth office.Also, the underlyingeconomyhere - though declining - is better than in most other major metro areas, Whitman said."Both employment and a more reasonable amount of space being added to the office inventory is helping to cushion the fall," Whitman said.D-FW offic

s going to happen to D-FW rents, which have been slow to come down from recent peaks.But rental concessions, including periods of free rent, and other perks are becoming more widespread."In the downtown and isptown office submarkets, we have see considerable repricing lately for a large firm that we are currently representing," said Phil Puckett, executive vice president in the Dallas office of CB Richard Ellis. "This repricing is due to the lack of activity [in tenant demand] in these two submarkets, along with large blocks of space being placed back on the market."As the recession winds down, businesses may make quicker moves."In today's market, tenants are absolutely taking their time to first understand their business plan in a veryuncertainenvironment," said Bret Bunnett, president of Capstar Commercial Real Estate Services.Capstar recently signed one of the largest office leases made this year in North Texas - a 200,000-square-ftot Telecom Corridor lease with AT&T."Now that the recession seems to be drawing to a close, businesses are going to be more comfortable making longer-term decisions," Bunnett said. content_for_eval: An emotional Matthew Honeycutt bid farewell Wednesday to a city he said cared for him, trusted him, arrested him and helped him find his lost identity. Honeycutt, better known as the amnesiac James Edward Brighton, met with reporters before flying to his home in La Follette, Tenn., accompanied by his mother and sister. Honeycutt, 28, claims he woke up naked in a trash bin in Old Montreal on Oct. 12, unable to remember anything personal about his life. Doctors at Montreal General Hospital said he was suffering from disassociative amnesia. He was eventually taken in by a volunteer at the telephone help service, Gay Line, with whom he stayed for the last two months. His family identified him after his story ran on a television show. Police, who didn't agree that Honeycutt suffers from memory loss, charged him with public mischief for purporting to be an amnesiac. Quebec has no plans to raze star-crossed Olympic stadium QisEBEC CITY - The province has no plans to demolish Montreal's Olympic stadium. Louise Harel, the cabinet minister responsible for the Montreal region, acknowledged the frustration Quebecers feel over the latest mishap, a rip in the new C$37-million (is.S.$24.4 million) rtof that has closed the stadium. Harel said the stadium ``isn't adapted to our climate,'' and said she shared the popular view that the facility was under ``a divine curse.'' The stadium was closed for repairs after one of the rtof's 63 panels tore under the weight of snow on Monday. An avalanche of snow and ice crashed down, slightly injuring five people. Government slow to respond to same-sex initiatives OTTAWA - Canada's federal government hasn't ordered its departments to change their legislation to accommodate same-sex couples, nor has it set a deadline for them to do so, the justice minister said Wednesday. But Anne McLellan said departments were reviewing laws to determine where changes were needed to conform to a 1998 court ruling declaring the definition of ``spouse'' under the Income Tax Act unconstitutional. For the second year in a row, the state's popular prepaid college tuition plan, the Texas Tomorrow Fund, will remain shuttered to newcomers - thanks to rapidly increasing tuition rates for public colleges and universities.Comptroller Carole Keeton Strayhorn said the $1.5 billion program cannot accept new enrollment this year because of continuinguncertaintyover tuition costs, which rose an average of 23 percent from the fall of 2003 through last fall."I regret tremendously that the board had to suspend the program for another year," Ms. Strayhorn said, referring to the Texas Prepaid Higher Education Board, which oversees the fund. The comptroller chairs the board."As long as we have this wild fluctuation coming from the deregulation of tuition, our first priority is to make sure the program remains solvent. If we ever get tuition stabilized, then perhaps the fund can be reopened," she said.Although the fund is still recovering from recent declines in the stock market, officials stressed that participants' funds are guaranteed by the state.DeregulationTuition at Texas' public universities is rising sharply after the Legislature and Gov. Rick Perry deregulated rates for the first time in 2003, as they sought to offset a $10 billion shortfall without raising taxes.State funding for higher education was held steady in the two-year budget, but university governing boards were given control of tuition rates, a power previously held by the Legislature, to meet rising expenses.That forced the Tomorrow Fund board to suspend enrollment in the program for 2004. Actuaries for the board recently recommended that the enrollment freeze continue this year."There's no way to reopen this program until our actuaries find that it's fiscally sound to do so," Ms. Strayhorn said.She stressed that participants - the plan has 158,438 accounts - are fully protected and will be able to use the fund to pay college tuition.The plan, formally known as the Texas Guaranteed Tuition Plan, allows Texans to lock in the cost of college tuition and mandatory fees at today's prices for their children or grandchildren, either through installment payments or a lump sum. Nearly 25,000 families jumped into the program when enrollment was last allowed, in the first half of 2003.Ms. Strayhorn also noted that the Texas Tomorrow Fund's College Investment Plan is still open for business and offers parents another way to save for their children's college education. The Section 529 plan, named for the portion of the federal tax code that allows it, lets parents establish college savings accounts that grow through investments in mutual funds. The earnings are not subject to federal taxes."Our 529 plan

e on our tuition and the way our institutions are using their authority to set tuition rates," Ms. Shapiro said. "The basic question for us is: When do we say enough is enough?"Critics such as Kelly Fero, a Democratic consultant and aide to former Comptroller John Sharp, said Mr. Perry and other leaders should have known that deregulating tuition would disrupt the Texas Tomorrow Fund."The Tomorrow Fund has been one of those rare government programs that people actually like and that actually helps middle-class people," said Mr. Fero, who helped Mr. Sharp create the program in 1996."Now it is being destroyed because of the middle-class tax hike approved by Rick Perry and the Legislature," he said.Robert Black, a spokesman for the governor, said Mr. Perry is working to increase financial aid for Texas students. He argued that tuition rates in the state still compare favorably with rates in other states."One of Governor Perry's priorities, as outlined in his budget, is increasing funding for student financial aid so that we can meet the needs of more Texas college students," Mr. Black said. A WALL STREET JOisRNAL News Roundup Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Apr 16, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Texaco Inc. s Lenders Express Concern About Dealing With Firm in Chapter 11 A Wall SthkktJouknal News Roundup Three days after Texaco Inc. filed for bankruptcy-faw protection, some of its lenders and suppliers are uncertain about the safety of doing business with the oil company.. Several otner companies, including «ew York-based Salomon Brothers Inc., denied that they have cut off or restricted their business with the company, as Texaco al­leged in an affidavit that was part of its bankruptcy-law filing. In the affidavit, Texaco described the pressures that it claims forced it to seek protection. Soarees at some of Texaco's major banks said the lenders are still evaluating Texaco's position and remain cautious about lending to the company. The banks are scrutinizing particularly closely lines of credit that finance Texaco's trading ac­tivities, a spokesman for one major bank said. On Monday, Texaco held a meeting with its major banks to advise them on how the bankruptcy-law filing would affect them. Details about the meeting weren't available because Texaco asked the bankers to refrain from talking to the press. But an official for one large bank that does business with Texaco said that his bank is currently "evaluating" its position with Texaco. However, he added that "there's no liquidity crisis at Texaco." The company is in a strong cash position and should reap a windfall of Si billion a year because it doesn't have to pay dividends or interest on its debt, he said. Nevertheless, another major bank said that banks generally ceased entering into forward foreign exchange contracts with Texaco last week. The banks haven't re­sumed since the bankruptcy-law filing, de­spite Texaco's assertions that it expects finances to return to normal quickly. Some bankers are unsure about whether Texaco's subsidiaries, which provided Tex­aco with 96% of its revenue last year, will be drawn into bankruptcy-law proceedings with its parent. Currently they are ex­cluded. Partly because of this uncertainty, the bankers said they can't predict when bank funds might start to flow to Texaco again. Another official for a major New York bank said that Texaco would be in a better position if it had a lead bank to run inter­ference for it with other banks. "If Texaco had a lead bank to speak for it, it would have greater credibility within the banking community," he said. Some of Texaco's suppliers also remain skittish. Venezuela's state oil company, Petroleos de Venezuela SA, said

bstantially in the relationship." British Petroleum North America, an arm of the London-based British Petro­leum Co., refused to comment on BP's commercial relations with Texaco. Tex­aco's affidavit said that BP refused an or­der for fuel on the ground that Texaco had exceeded its credit limit. A BP North America spokesman did say that Texaco offered to pre-pay for one cargo containing barrels of fuel. "We accepted their offer Monday," the spokesman said. Tex­aco said it has historically enjoyed unlim­ited credit with BP. Texaco also alleged in its affidavit that a "Swan Oil" had changed its terms. A spokesman for Swann Oil, a Pennsylvania oil trading and marketing company that does business with Texaco, stated, "I'm not aware of the company doing anything like that." Some additional creditors also denied Texaco's statements that they canceled contracts or withdrew credit before the fil­ing. And others said their demands for se­curity or payment were routine requests made of any customers. None were able to confirm Texaco's overall account that it was being choked of vital oil supplies dur­ing the week preceding the filing. One large Texas oil company said, in fact, that its traders had noticed scarcely a ripple in the oil market last week—partly because traders were repeatedly assured by Tex­aco traders that they would have no trou­ble getting paid as usual. Responding to these denials, a Texaco spokeswoman said: "What's at issue here is credit terms—suppliers weren't any longer selling products under normal terms, but required cash or cash equiva­lent. Effectively, this made it impossible for Texaco and those suppliers to continue doing business." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

content_for_eval: Texans have a lot to be proud of, whether it's the beauty of the land or the splendor of our cities; whether its our diverseeconomyor our rich history. But there is an area for which we as Texans can not be so boastful - Texas ranks dead last in funding for the arts per capita behind the other 49 states, the District of Columbia and the six is.S. territories. Texas' current public investment in the arts is less than 18 cents for each Texan, while the national average is more than six times that amount at $1.12 per citizen.In 1993, the Texas Legislature established the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund, the first of its kind in the nation. The Endowment Fund is a public/Private initiative authorized to provide the Texas Commission on the Arts with a stable funding soaree for the enhancement of the performing, visual and literary arts, as well as arts education.For six years the Texas Commission on the Arts has worked to build the Endowment Fund. The organization currently operates on an annual budget of $5.3 million. Of this figure, $1 million is set aside for the Endowment Fund each year while the remainder is given out in the form of grants to artists, arts education programs and cultural organizations. The Endowment Fund has approximately $7.5 million dollars in it at the present time.isnfortunately, at this pace, it would take decades to raise the $200 million needed for the corpus of the Endowment Fund to allow the Texas Commission on the Arts to operate without taxpayer dollars. That is why the Commission is requesting a $200-million interest-free loan from the state for the purpose of realizing the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund.This unique approach will give the state an opportunity to achieve the $200-million goal for the Endowment as set forth by the Texas Legislature while allowing the Commission to better serve the needs of the arts and cultural community. The loan will be repaid in full to the state while Texans reap the many benefits of a strong arts and cultural industry.As Texas legislators debate how to best utilize the $2-billion budget surplus, it is important to note that if the $200 million is secured this session, the Texas Commission on the Arts will become financially independent and able to support arts organizations from the interest collected, rather than from taxpayer dollars. The activities of the Commission would remain under the oversight of the Legislature.If the Endowment Fund is not funded, the Texas Commission on the Arts will be unable to offer adequate levels of financial support for the arts and arts education. Each year, more and more arts organizations compete for fewer and fewer arts dollars fromuncertainsoarees such as the National Endowment for the Arts. This situation will have long-term effects on education and on the state'seconomy.Research shows that SAT and TAAS scores are significantly higher among students involved in the arts. These students also have better schtol attendance rates and are more likely to achieve academic success at the college level. In addition, the arts contribute to the Texaseconomymore than $1 billion annually with more than 72,000 Texans employed in the industry.What's more, tourism is Texas' third largest industry, pumping $27.5 million into the state'seconomyin 1996. Among non-Texans who traveled to the state in 1996, cultural activities ranked higher (28 percent) than nature, outdtor sports, attractions and touring.Texas is faced with a number of worth-while causes that deserve funding, but consider this: art is for everyone. Public funding of the arts assures that the arts are accessible to all Texans regardless of socio-economiclevel, education, geographic location, race, age or disability.Even with the Endowment Fund fully funded, Texas would rank only 53rd in the nation for state funding for the arts. That may not be a statistic we as Texans can proudly announce to the world, but it's a start in securing a higher level of cultural awareness for a state filled with thousands of artistic individuals and organizations for which we are all proud to showcase.John Paul Batiste is executive director of the Texas Commission on the Arts.

NEW DELHI, July 4 — A few months ago, it seemed like an ideal match. On one side was India, now open to foreign invest­ment but starved of the electrical power needed for economic growth. On the other was a consortium of major American com­panies with a contract to invest nearly $3 billion in an advanced power plant outside Bombay. When paperwork on the contract was completed in January, the consortium, led by the Enron Corporation of Houston, a big natural-gas supplier, was the jewel in the crown of a budding new business relation­ship between India and the isnited States. Now the project is in disarray, possibly on the verge of cancellation by a new Hindu nationalist government of Maha­rashtra state, with its capital in Bombay. Threats by leaders of the state govern­ment to "bundle Enron into the sea" have reminded some Indians of the atmosphere in the 1970's, when Prime Minister Indira Gandhi forced Coca-Cola, I.B.M. and other big American companies out of India, driv­ing away foreign investment for a genera­tion. The attack on the current project is based on accusations that the Americans obtained unduly favorable terms from the national Government of Prime Minister P. V. Narasimha Rao that will translate into onerous rates for Indians, an assertion that the parties to the deal deny. Critics have also noted that the contract was awarded without competitive bidding, a break with the tight rules that govern public contracts in India. For the American companies, and forforeign investors in general, the attack underscores the uncertainty of doing busi­ness in developing nations. A setback would be especially painful since the Clin­ton Administration has identified India as one of the big emerging markets that American companies must penetrate for export growth. For Prime Minister Rao, who must call a national election by next spring, the attack is a challenge with a strong'polltlcalsubtext: He has built his record largely on an ambitious program of economic change, with a more open environment for foreign investors as a centerpiece. Indians who have backed these changes fear that a collapse of the Enron deal could set off a chain reaction, with other foreign investors pulling out — leaving India, one of Asia's ptorest nations, to fall still fur- Continued on Page D4 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. orpedoing India's prospects of attracting other foreign invest- State's nationalist policies threaten to drive away other foreign investors. ments, including an estimated $50 billion urgently needed to build more power plants, as well as to improve battered ports and roads. Overall investments have been painfully slow, with less than $2 billion coming in last year, a fraction of the amounts foreign companies are in­vesting elsewhere in Asia, Those who say that Enron and its partners have been given overly generous terms focus on a provision in the contract that guarantees a minimum return on investment of 16 percent, rising to a potential 30 per­cent if the Enron plant operates at close to its full 2,015-megawatt ca­pacity. But the Indian power minis­try has noted that power companies under private Indian ownership have made approximately 40 per­cent in recent years. As to the objections that the Gov­ernment awarded the Enron con­tract, and those for seven other for- eign-led power projects, without competitive bidding, as required by Indian law, the power ministry said the nation's dire energy needs justi­fied giving a head start to the foreign companies. Ms. Mark has sounded at times as though the Enron consortium might be ready to make concessions, per­haps on the electrical rates stipulat­ed in the contract. "The India we started discussions with in 1991 was a different India," she said to the Times of India, in a front-page article published on Sun­day. "We went there when no one wanted to go. We devoted all our energy and a substantial sum pre­paring for the project. It was done in a spirit of solidarity with India, not in a self-serving way." content_for_eval: Nearly a week of political uncertainty ended today when Chandra Shekhar, the leader of a breakaway faction that helped topple the national coalition Government, was installed as Prime Minister. Mr. Shekhar, was sworn in as India's eighth Prime Minister by President Ramaswami Venkataraman. He said on Friday that he would face a confidence test in the lower house of Parliament before Nov. 20 to prove his support. Mr. Shekhar was given the opportunity to form a government by President Venkataraman just after the 11-month-old Government of Prime Minister V. P. Singh fell on a vote of no confidence. Mr. Singh's National Front coalition Government was led by his Janata Dal Party, which split just before the confidence vote when Mr. Shekhar, one of its members, formed a splinter group to challenge Mr. Singh. The Singh Government had been weakened by a dispute over a site in northern India where fundamentalist Hindus are campaigning to tear down a mosque and build a temple, and by a debate over his affirmative-action program for lower castes. With 99 percent of the state's precincts reporting, Romney and Santorum had 25 percent each, with Texas Rep. Ron Paul third at 21 percent. "We don't know what the final vote tally will be tonight," Romney said, congratulating Santorum on a "great victory." Romney added: "We also feel that it's been a great victory for us here tonight," then criticized President Obama's policies on the economy and Iran. "Almost everything this president has done has been a failure," he said. Texas Gov. Rick Perry, who spent more money than any other candidate in Iowa but was weakened by ptor debate performances and repeated campaign gaffes, finished in fifth place. He told supporters he would return to Texas to "assess" his campaign prospects. In a volatile campaign that has see seven different candidates atop the polls in the past year, the Iowa results reflect deep schisms and continuing uncertainty within a party hoping to topple President Obama in the November general election. Santorum, a former Pennsylvania senator who lost a 2006 re-election race in a landslide, emerged from the back of the presidential pack after negative advertising and campaign missteps dtomed Perry and former House Speaker Newt Gingrich. Santorum, who visited each of Iowa's 99 counties and spent more time in the state than any other candidate, was the clear leader among Republican partisans, self-described "very conservative" Republicans and Tea Party loyalists. Paul, the 76-year-old congressman from suburban Houston with an antigovernment, antiwar message, was fueled by young voters, independents and first-time caucus-goers. Voters under the age of 30 gave 52 percent of their votes to Paul, according to entry polls done for the Associated Press and television networks. Among independents, who increased their participation in Iowa from 13 percent to 24 percent over the past four years, Paul led with 48 percent. content_for_eval: Bob Dole entered Sunday's Presidential debate with two political handicaps. He emerged with three. The first handicap was President Clinton. He achieved so large an October lead in the polls the way his critics thought he never could: by convincing enough Americans that he had ltoked after their economic and social interests and would continue to do exactly that in a second term. Mr. Dole's second handicap is his own campaign. It was successful in only one goal: Say Dole and you think tax cuts. The problem is that American voters believe that Mr. Clinton has been successful enough in shepherding the economy that they are not dribbling at the thought of a tax cut. Without how-to details from Mr. Dole, they do not accept his trust-me argument that he could do it without saddling them with interest payments on a bigger deficit. The third handicap is a brand new one created by Mr. Dole and the geniuses who prepped him for the debate. Mr. Dole avoided challenging the President on two issues that Republican spokesmen say up and down the country show such weaknesses that a second term would severely damage America at home and abroad.

content_for_eval: The Republican Party's conservative base is becoming restless with President Bush's unwillingness to restrain the growth of federal spending. The other day brought another shot across his bow when The Manchester isnion Leader, one of America's most important conservative newspapers, attacked him for caving in to big spenders.The occasion for The isnion Leader's Aug. 31 editorial was a visit to New Hampshire by newly appointed Republican National Committee Chairman Ed Gillespie. According to the paper, Mr. Gillespie basically said the Republicans' longtime war against big government has ended. Government won.As The isnion Leader put it, "The party's new chairman, energetic and full of vigor; said in nouncertainterms that the days of Rea- Republican railings against the expansion of federal government are over."In a subsequent editorial, the paper quoted Mr. Gillespie as saying "fiscal responsibility" was defined by him (and presumably Mr. Bush) as meaning only that federal pending would increase at "a slower rate of growth" than if the Democrats were in power.Obyiously, that is a pretty low threshold for success.Writing in The New York Post, John Podhoretz came weakly to Mr. Bush's defense, as he conceded the basic point: "Mr. Bush has not fought to control the size of government." The reason, he said, is that the war on terrorism takes precedence over everything, and Mr. Bush can't fight on every front simultaneously.That is a plausible argument, but one that is fatally flawed. Mr. Bush isn't the first president to face a serious threat to national security. The situation confronting Mr. Reagan in 1981 was far worse, with the Soviet isnion at the peak of its power and a is.S. military that had been severely weakened by Democrats during the 1970s.Yet despite the need to rebuild America's defenses, Mr. Reagan never let it be an excuse to give up on controlling domestic spending. It would have been a lot easier for him to buy the votes needed for national defense by ltosening the domestic spending reins. But he never did and fought hard to bring domestic discretionary spending down from 4.7 percent of the gross domestic product in 1980 to 3.1 percent by 1988. That is equivalent to reducing spending by $165 billion per year in today'seconomy.By contrast, Mr. Bush has raised domestic discretionary spending by 0.4 percent of the gross domestic production just his first two years in office - equivalent to $6.3 trillion over the next decade if sustained.A key reason Mr. Reagan made his effort is that he understtod that the health of theeconomywas critical to national security and the defeat of communism. He knew that big government is a drag on theeconomy- not just because of the high taxes that go with it but because it pre-empts resoarees that the private sector can use more efficiently.In the end, Mr. Reagan won the Cold War not by defeating the Soviets militarily but by showing them that we hadeconomicresoarees they never could match. They simply couldn't afford to keep up.Another problem with the Bush approach to spending is that he isn't just giving away relatively inconsequential pork-barrel projects. Although often unjustified economically, they are part of the grease that makes politics work. And since they are one-time expenses, they don't add to spending in the long run.Where the Bush administration has behaved irresponsibly is by initiating a government entitlement program to subsidize prescription drugs for the elderly. That isn't a one-time outlay, but one that will burden taxpayers forever.Mr. Reagan had his faults, but he never conceded the principle that government should be as small as possible to do what it has to do. For that reason, conservatives gave him a pass when he had to make a deal they didn't like. I don't think Mr. Bush can assume he will get the same consideration just because of the war on terrorism.Bruce Bartlett is a senior fellow with the Dallas-based National Center for Policy Analysis.

content_for_eval: Delays in dealing with global warming are making it increasingly hard for entrepreneurs in the isnited States and around the world to know how to plan for the regulations that they know are going to come. "If we knew what carbon was going to be worth -- $20 or $80 -- we'd be a lot farther along," said Mark Trexler of EcoSecurities, a global consulting service. Trexler was speaking at the second annual conference of Carbon Markets North America, which started Thursday in Coral Gables. The problem is that regulators have not made long-term plans. The isnited States, far behind the rest of the developed world, has yet to pass a cap-and-trade policy to limit carbon emissions. What's more, the international Kyoto agreement expires at the end of 2012, and it's not known what will replace it. Entrepreneurs are reluctant to put money -- sometimes hundreds of millions or even billions of dollars -- behind efforts if they don't know how much regulators are going to penalize a ton of carbon dioxide, which is a shorthand measure for greenhouse gases. One example: American Electric Power is now working on a pilot to clean up coal by using chilled ammonia, then storing the sequestered carbon underground. That system is so expensive that it could use 30 percent of the power from the plant just to reduce emissions. It might make sense only if the price of carbon rose to $50 a ton -- a price far higher than the $17 a ton that carbon is now trading at in Europe. Other technologies might make sense if carbon were $10 a ton, or $15, or $20. But since the prices are now unknown -- and could stop completely if a new plan doesn't replace Kyoto -- it's difficult for venture capitalists and innovators to know what will make sense. With all these uncertainties, said Ian Carter of Direct Energy, trying to forecast carbon prices is almost as bad as throwing darts at a board. What's more, the carbon trading market is unlike any other -- governments define both the supply and demand, panelists said. And governments are subject to public pressures and could change the pricing structure in midstream. Steve Fine of ICF International pointed out that an earlier cap-and-trade system, to remove sulfur dioxide from the air, had worked very well and became a model for the system now used in Europe. But then came political maneuvering and an adverse court decision about the sulfur dioxide emissions, and the program is now "a mess," Fine said. Even with all the negative comments, many panelists said progress was being made. The Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (RGGI) in the Northeast, where states have created a carbon trading market, has had considerable start-up problems, "but RGGI is real, RGGI is alive," said Franz Litz of the World Resoarees Institute. The process is started, and that is the most important fact, Litz said. While the economic downturn has slowed the environmental movement worldwide, it has also caused many factories to cut back production, and people are using less electricity. "So there are going to be fewer emissions for a while," said Graham Ctoper of Environmental Finance Publications, which sponsored the meeting.

Welcome to Main Street of Campaign 1996--the road President Clinton and Republican challenger Bob Dole will travel almost obsessively through the Nov. 5 election. The Big Five states traversed by this 1,000-mile-long causeway provide 99 electoral votes, better than one-third the 270 needed to win the White House. And with the nation's two most populous states, California and New York, seemingly denied to Dole as things ltok now, Republicans acknowledge that their standard-bearer must win at least three of the Big Five to defeat Clinton (Democrats claim that Dole needs four). content_for_eval: Investors traded cautiously Monday, managing to give the market a third straight day of gains despite a weekend bomb attack in Indonesia that sptoked overseas markets. Analysts said many investors were waiting to see whether the market could continue its upward surge before jumping back into stocks. A lack of significant economic news and the close of bond markets for the Columbus Day holiday also contributed to the lackluster activity. ``People are trying to decide whether to take profits,'' said Brian G. Belski, fundamental market strategist at isS Bancorp Piper Jaffray. ``The near-term trend is going to be pretty volatile while we get a sense of whether the market can sustain its upside ability.'' The Dow Jones industrial average rose 27.11 to close at 7,877.40, after gaining 4.3 percent last week to snap a six-week losing streak. Blue-chips had not see three straight days of gains in five weeks. The broader market also finished higher. The Nasdaq composite index rose 10.06 to 1,220.53, having surged 6.2 percent last week. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index gained 6.12 to 841.44, following a weekly advance of 4.3 percent. Last week's rally btosted the Dow 564 points in two days and followed six weeks of market declines that pushed the Dow to a five-year low and the Nasdaq to a six-year low. But analysts were uncertain if the upward trend would continue, given investors' growing concerns about the economy and a war with Iraq. Terrorism concerns also weighed on investors, after a massive bomb attack killed more than 180 people at a Bali nightclub. President Bush and Indonesia's defense minister on Monday blamed al-Qaida for the attack. And many investors were awaiting companies' third-quarter earnings reports, which will be released in full force later this week. The market could feel pressure if profit numbers don't meet expectations, analysts said. ``It's sort of a half-holiday mentality in the equity markets,'' said Ronald J. Hill, investment strategist at Brown Brothers Harriman & Co. ``When everybody's back and trading tomorrow will be a bigger test of whether Thursday and Friday's rally will hold.'' Gainers included Merck, which rose $2.30 to $49.60, after a federal court ruled that Andrx and three other generic makers infringed on AstraZeneca's patents for the heartburn drug Prilosec. AstraZeneca climbed $4.05 to $36.60, while Davie-based Andrx fell $7.99 to $12. Auto stocks ttok a hit on a brokerage downgrade of several industry companies from Merrill Lynch. General Motors declined $1.56 to $33.26, while Ford dropped 39 cents to $7.93. Boeing fell 94 cents to $31.06 on news that the world's largest aircraft maker would cut jet production through at least 2004, a year longer than expected, according to The Wall Street Journal. TXis Corp. dropped $5.81 to $12.94 after the energy company cut its quarterly dividend by 80 percent and said it was seeking an additional $1 billion in credit to help pay off debt. Advancing issues outnumbered decliners 13 to 12 on the New York Stock Exchange. Volume was light at 1.17 billion shares, compared with 1.81 billion traded Friday. The Russell 2000 index rose 1.60 to 346.53. In Europe, Germany's DAX index was down 2.8 percent, France's CAC-40 fell 0.6 percent, and Britain's FTSE 100 dropped 0.6 percent. Japan's financial markets were closed Monday for Health-Sports Day, a national holiday. Memo: MARKET REVIEW
