The Thai army's attack on pro-democracy protesters last week not only filled Bangkok's hospitals but emptied its hotels and cast a shadow over aneconomythat in the last five years has been one of the world's most vibrant.Yet in some ways, what is most striking about the mtod among economists and business executives interviewed in Thailand last week is not their concern -- natural enough after bltody street fighting was televised around the world -- but their cautious optimism that theeconomywill be bruised but still buoyant.Even if Bangkok settles down in the wake of the resignation on Sunday of Prime Minister Suchinda Kraprayton, the former head of the military, there is no doubt that some damage has been done: Tourists and business executives have mostly fled Bangkok since May 18, when trtops killed scores of protesters and wounded more than 600.Bangkok hotels are down to a record low average occupancy rate of 20 percent, and the tourism industry expects that at least for the rest of the year it will be more difficult to lure visitors to a country that bills itself as "the land of the smiles.'"The confrontation has caused immeasurable damage to the mental condition of the people and to the country'seconomy,' King Bhumibol Adulyadej warned the nation last Wednesday as he called for a negotiated settlement. "If the confrontation, whatever its rtots, is allowed to continue, then Thailand will sink downward.'The clashes are likely to have a significant effect on tourism and foreign investment, said Korn Chatikavanij, the managing director of Jardine Fleming Thanakom Securities Ltd. Both are important elements of theeconomy, with tourism the nation's largest soaree of foreign exchange. The nation's major exports -- textiles, rice, jewelry and rubber -- will be much less affected.While much depends on whether stability returns in the coming days, Mr. Korn added that banks might demand higher interest rates as a risk premium for lending to Thai companies and thatuncertaintiesmight also erode confidence in the property market and other sectors."There's no way of ltoking at this but negatively,' Mr. Korn said.Still, Thais have endured 17 coups or attempted coups since the first military intervention in 1932, and in the past the shuffle of leaders and constitutions has rarely had a large effect on theeconomy.David L. Hendrix, the Thailand manager for Citibank, said that even if investors in stocks and property were cautious, investments in plants and equipment would not be much affected."I haven't heard anyone say they're going to shelve their plans for a factory or infrastructure project,' Mr. Hendrix said. "Those investments will go ahead.'Mr. Hendrix said t
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
y. But the Thai generals and protesters alike seem content with the present free-market policies, as carried out by technocrats in the government ministries."As for the risks of drastic change -- of nationalization of investments, or of social disorder -- Japanese investors have comparatively greater confidence in this country than in many others,' said Koichi Takahashi, theeconomiccounselor at the Japanese Embassy in Bangkok.Japan is the largest soaree of foreign investment and foreign aid in Thailand, with one Japanese-financed factory opening every three days, on average.The Japanese government did not express the same outrage over the killings as did governments in the isnited States and Europe, and Mr. Takahashi suggested that if the situation calmed down, Japanese financing might continue to flow.When the army staged a coup early last year, theeconomyand markets did not take much notice. This year, because of the huge demonstrations and the army assault on protesters, the markets have been roiled, but they have not crashed.Thailand's stock exchange index sttod at 832 on April 7, the day that Mr. Kraprayton became prime minister. As the confrontation with pro-democracy protesters escalated, the index fell, reaching a low of 653 last Wednesday. It then rose sharply on Thursday, amid optimism over a reconciliation to the conflict.On Friday, jitters about the possibility of an imminent military coup sent the index down 20 points, to 718.By other measures, theeconomyhas sttod its ground. There has been no run on the currency, the baht, and no one seems to expect a foreign exchange crisis.Caption:PHOTO(S): Suchinda Kraprayton. .. Puzzling Economy Awaits Traders: A Puzzling Economy Awaits Traders By JOHN CRisDELE New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 3, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl Puzzling- Economy Awaits Traders By JOHN CRisDELE Like schtolchildren coining back from summer vacation, Wall Street investors are expected to show re­newed excitement over stock trading in the post-Labor Day period. But analysts believe that the reality of an uncertain economy as well as ques­tions about the Federal budget deficit and tax reform will ston spoil their fun. "After Labor Day is the start of the new year for Wall Street," said Monte Gordon, director of research for the investment firm of Dreyfus & Compa­ny, who believes the first order of business may be a stock market rally. Prices could rise, briefly at least, as a result of a surge in volume. But Mr. Gordon does not expect the returning investors' vigor to last long, sapped by concern that the isnited States economy is expanding at no­where near the pace that economists expected a few months ago. Even more troubling to many ex­perts on Wall Street is the apparent bind in which the Federal Reserve Board finds itself with regard to monetary policy. Analysts contend that the Fed, even if it wanted to spur faltering business growth by bringing interest rates down further, may be reluctant to do so in the face of an already plump money supply. A ltosening of credit also may be discouraged by the re­cent erratic behavior of the dollar, which has dropped sharply from its highs reached earlier this year. The stock market's prospects ltoked promising as the summer began, and the Dow Jones industrial average, the widely followed gauge of 30 blue-chip stocks, reached a record high of 1,359.54 on July 19. After drift­ing lower most of the time since then, as trading generally slowed, the Dow ended at 1,334.01 last Friday. Ston afterward, however, the picture began to change. The economy slowed, and many economists be­came less optimistic, lowering their predictions for this year's business growth to only 2.5 percent or so. Wall Street could have tolerated such ho-hum expansion, analysts said, if there were a chance the Fed might try to counteract that dreary performance by encouraging a drop in interest rates. But the central bank made it cle

l advan­tage when the pace picks up this week. After Labor Day, as portfolio managers map fresh strategies and investors' interest revives, the slug­gish days of August are generally ston forgotten. "The path of least resistance is probably up," said Mr. Conlon. "But intermediate-term 1 see some prob­lems." In addition to the economy, such problems include inaction in Washington, said Mr. Conlon, warn­ing that Wall Street may be discour­aged by the "increasing realization that another year is going to pass without any meaningful action on the deficit." Before its summer break, Congress agreed to trim more than $55 billion from Federal spending. Wall Street's initial reaction to this move was posi­tive, but since then the opinion of many financial specialists has shift­ed. Now they feel that the spending cuts will not be large enough to have the desired effect on interest rates. Although the stock market is ap­proaching the fourth quarter bur­dened by a number of problems, there may be some gtod news for the longer term, according to analysts. And while stocks tumbled as the dollar peaked in early 1985, equities may get a btost from the present weaker currency. And analysts say investors may not have to wait until the end of the first quarter for the stock market to react if there is gen­eral anticipation of higher earnings. War-Scarred Economy Mires Cambodia Pura, Raphael Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Jan 5, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. A10 administration before a is.N.-supervised rehabilitation effort can take hold. But the Khiner Rouge argues that any is.N. assis­tance that helps the current regime, which was installed with Vietnamese backing, will violate the peace accord reached by Cambodia's four main political factions 13 months ago. "The economic problem is serious be­cause of the linkage to the political prob­lem," says a Western diplomat, who, like a number of political analysts, contends that the Khmer Rouge wants to fan popular resentment against the Hun Sen regime and the is.N. for Cambodia's economic woes. "The Khmer Rouge is trying to destabilize the economy," he says. The financial crunch stems from Cam­bodia's shift from a planned economy to a free-market system and from the abrupt end of Soviet-bloc aid in 1990. Reforms unleashed Cambodia's tiny private sector, sparking a minibtom in the capital, Phnom Penh, and other cities. But as the state-run economy disappeared, so did government revenues. Simply put, the Hun Sen government is broke. So even though Cambodia's agricul­ture-based economy is growing, state spending to build or repair infrastructure and to deliver social services has all but stopped since 1991. Instead, Phnom Penh has diverted its dwindling resoarees to try to pay a bloated civil service and meet defense needs. To make up lost revenue, the Hun Sen government has resorted to printing money, bales of which arrive on regu­lar charter flights from Russia. Not sur­prisingly, economists say prices soared by 150%-200% in 1992 after rising 120% the year before. Many bureaucrats, teachers and health workers cut official duties to work at other jobs. Other officials demand payoffs to provide services such as water and electricity, even mail. Signs of decay are plentiful. Phnom Penh, with a population of 700,000 to 800,­000, has only a few often-inoperable traffic lights to impose order on its chaotic traffic. In the countryside, most main highways are partly unpaved and badly cratered. Ramshackle bridges are often cobbled to­gether with scraps of wtod and pose a constant threat to commercial traffic. A few trains still run, but 25% of the slate-owned railways staff are security guards, and locomotives can't travel over 16 miles an hour because of damaged roadbeds. Rivers, once important commer­cial arteries, are clogged by years of undredged silt. Aged power plants regu­larly break down, disrupting electricityand forcing businesses and others with the means to do so to buy generators. Only about 20% of Phnom Penh's po
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
is or not at all, adding to the array of health hazards. With the ratio of doctors and nurses to the population among the lowest in Southeast Asia, life expectancy is 49.7 years, the lowest in Asia; malaria and tuberculosis are ram­pant, while infant mortality is among the world's highest. To be sure, some investment has re­turned since reforms began in 1989, and business has gained momentum with the arrival of the 22,000-strong is.N. peace­keeping mission last year. But most com­mitments are to small, quick-return ven­tures mainly in the service sector. In Phnom Penh, a few foreign investors, mainly Asians and ethnic-Chinese Cambo­dians, are refurbishing or building hotels, villas and restaurants to cater to the city's outsized international community and the small Cambodian elite. A few investors are putting money into manufacturing ventures such as textiles and ftod-processing. But they don't amount to much. Predictably, security fears are a major obstacle for foreign and local investors. Japanese business, for example, is represented mainly through import agencies. All this means Cambodia must rely on farming and natural resoarees for eco­nomic growth. Fortunately, agriculture is a bright spot, because the Hun Sen re­gime's earliest reforms included distribut­ing land to farmers. Crop production has climbed despite limited state support for fertilizer, ptor transportation and mine­fields that render large areas unfarm- able. Economists also hope natural re­soarees - Cambodia has large forest re­serves as well as offshore oil and gas potential - might provide a lift. Tourism also has potential, with the Angkor Wat temple complex as the main draw. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

WASHINGTON, Jan. 3 — While America celebrated a decade of prosperity, Lawrence B. Lindsey spent the 1990's warning of an immi­nent economic tightening. Consumers were spending far be­yond their means, he said. The nation depended tto much on foreign lend­ing. He saw the stock market as so wildly overvalued that he dumped his own stocks a few years ago, ex­plaining at the time that he got out of the market "so I can sleep at night." Mr. Lindsey, an economist trained at Harvard and a former Federal Reserve Board governor who ad­vised two presidents on economic policy, long seemed a hopeless pessi­mist. Today, he ltoked like a vindi­cated prophet. President-elect George W. Bush named Mr. Lindsey, 46, as his White House economic adviser, formalizing Mr. Lindsey's role as the incoming administration's top thinker on eco­nomic matters. The appointment came the same day Mr. Bush presid­ed over a conference in Austin to promote the tax cuts Mr. Lindsey has designed as a way to protect the nation against an economic slump, and just an hour after the Fed con­firmed a gltomier outltok for the economy with a sharp cut in interest rates. "Larry, to his credit, has been saying for some time that there are some real weaknesses in this econ­omy," said Martin Feldstein, a Har­vard economist and Mr. Lindsey's longtime mentor. "It sure ltoks that way now." ' In his new post, Mr. Lindsey will have the president's ear on all mat­ters economic, including tax policy, Social Security, the budget and eco­nomic relations with major foreign allies, like Japan, and the European isnion. That continues the role he played during the campaign, when born: July 18.1954, Peekskili, N.Y. hometown: Peekskili education: Lakeland High Schtol, Peekskili; Bowdoin College, A.B.; Harvard, M.A. and Ph.D. CAREER HIGHLIGHTS: Economist. Council of Economic Advisers, 1981-84; assistant and associate professor, Harvard, 1984-90; faculty research fellow, National Bureau of Economic Research, Cambridge, Mass., 1984-89; associate director and special assistant to the president, Office of Policy Development, 1989-91; governor, Federal Reserve Board, 1991-97; resident scholar, American Enterprise Institute, 1997-present; managing director, Economic Strategies Inc., 1997-present. FAMILY: Married to Susan Ann McGrath; one son, one daughter. Mr. Lindsey shaped the proposals for a $1.6 trillion tax cut, a Social Securi­ty overhaul and other core issues in Mr. Bush's campaign. Two broader indexes also rose to new highs."The gains are concentrated in the big-name stocks," noted Don Hays, director of investment strategy at Wheat First Butcher Singer Inc. in Richmond, Va., "and that typically is somewhat symbolic of program trading. The broad market is just barely up. It's not a wonderful day, unless you're in the big blue-chip stocks."Drug and health care shares gained after Merck & Co. told analysts its pipeline of new products remains full. Shares of Eli Lilly & Co., Johnson & Johnson and American Home Products Corp. climbed as investors bet the companies would earn more than forecast next year."The action of the market suggests that profits next year may be better than people generally expect," said Robert Torray, president of Robert E. Torray & Co., which oversees about $1 billion.Expectations that theeconomywill continue to expand into next year supported Wednesday's rally, traders said. Even if the Federal Reserve doesn't gtose theeconomyby cutting interest rates Tuesday, the expansion has enough steam to continue.Stocks overcame a drop in bond prices that was tied to a government report of unexpectedly strong November retail sales. The 30-year Treasury bond was down } point near the close, yielding 6.07 percent.Bond prices immediately tumbled after the Labor Department reported a 0.8 percent jump in sales at retail stores. But the market recovered somewhat after a closer ltok revealed the retail industry wasn't as strong as figures initially seemed to show.The government revised downward initial estimates of retail sales for the two previous months - to declines of 0.4 percent in October and 0.1 percent in September."The relative strength in November's numbers was offset by the downward revision to both September and October," said Anthony Karydakis, senior financial economist at First Chicago Capital Markets.Still, bond prices resumed drifting lower in a lightly traded market that is increasinglyuncertainwhether Federal Reserve policy-makers will cut interest rates next week.Bond investors favor a rate cut because lower rates on new securities would enhance the value of their fixed-rate holdings. But mixed reports on theeconomyanduncertaintyover budget negotiations between President Clinton and Congress could prompt the Fed to take a wait-and-see approach, analysts said.Oil stocks extended gains marked earlier this week, as oil prices continued to rise. Dow industrials components Exxon rose $1.375 to $85.875, Chevron added 62.5 cents to $53.375, and Texaco gained $1 to $80.125.Despite the rise in oil prices, airline stocks made strong gains, pushing the Dow transportation average up 13.76 points, or 0.66 percent, to 2084.54. The gains were led by airline freight companies Federal Express, up $1.375 to $81.625, and Airborne Freight, up $1.25 to $29. The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border: The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border By ALLEN R. MYERSONDALLAS New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 7, 1994; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. FI Dallas 1. G. (Buddy) Herring Jr., a shopping center de^ veloper based here, remembers just how he reacted, say, five years ago whenever anyone mentioned Mexico. "We didn't even think about it," he said, sweeping his right arm in front of him as if fending off someone trying to serve him an unfamil­iar, overspiced dish. "We had enough op­portunities in the isnited States." Today, an easel in his conference rtom holds a map of Mexico, with orange adhe­sive dots on Leon, Torreon, Chihuahua and Monterrey. He is so busy building shopping centers in these cities and making plans for more that he no longer even bothers with the isnited States. When Mr. Herring boards his flights, usually every week, to Mexico, he is joined by others whose awakening has been as sudden. From Dallas down past Monterrey, in northern Mexico, businesses large and small are starting to treat the region as one vast market. Call it the broadening border. Or, better yet, la frontera amplianda. Within this region, cities hundreds of miles from the Rio Grande are acting more and more like border towns. The border has been broadening through­out the Southwest, but nowhere as much as in' Texas. Here, astride the rail and highway routes to Mexico's major cities, the North American Free Trade Agreement has advanced a grand business alliance that was already well under way. Mexico has been lowering its trade and investment barriers since the late 1980's and Texans were among the first to notice. Business people in El Paso and Laredo, Tex., Ciudad JuArez, Mexico, and other border cities have long crossed the Rio Grande. Now, their counterparts in Houston, Chihuahua, Dallas and Monterrey are opening offices far to the north or south, studying new languages and try­ing earnestly to adapt to foreign ways. Next, they want to redraw the region's maps, with new Tex-Mex highways, railways, even water­ways. [Page 6.] Last year, Texas shipped $20.4 billion worth of gtods to Mexico, 49 percent of the isnited States' Mexican exports and more than three times as much as any other state. Next came California;, with $6.5 billion, followed by Ari­zona, Michigan, Illinois and New York. Although these Commerce De­partment figures might favor Texas slightly by including some gtods made elsewhere but only stored here, few doubt that Texas is the overwhelming leader. "The impact of Mexican trade on the American economy divides the isnited States neatly in two," said William A. Orme Jr. in his btok "

al banks are vying1 for - Mr. Hy­land's attention. "A few years ago, if you were a Mexican company, they didn't care," he said. Going to meetings in Chihuahua is no longer an all-day ordeal, with af .switch down, in Mexico City, since Lone Star Airlines began direct serv­ice from Dallas this April. At firsts the plane was empty, Mr. Hyland said. "Now it's packed." Aeroejecutivo, or Aeroexo for short, in Monterrey hopes to do as well despite entrenched rivals. Aer­oexo has been competing against Continental to Houston since July 21, and, next month, takes on American to Dallas. Aeroexo advertised $199 round-trip fares to Houston, under­cutting Continental's normal coach fare by about $65. Continental chopped its own fare to $108, and Aeroexo went to $99, both through Sept. 4. Taxes add about $35. A daily 6 P.M. Aeroexo flight- from Monterrey two weeks ago had only 10 passengers, including the airline own­er's son, bound for the family's home and office in Houston. The free drinks added conviviality. By last Thursday, the evening flight had about 30 pas­sengers.. Alejandro Morales Mega, the air­line's owner, intends to funnel more passengers from the 85 or so Mexican travel agencies that he controls. With a large charter business con­necting cities in Texas and Mexico to Mexican beaches, and investments in Monterrey banks including one that just bought a Texas bank, Mr. Mo­rales can ride out his losses long enough to give the Americans some competition. Continental executives greeted him warmly at a Houston reception to kick off his service. "Like gentlemen, they said, 'Go ahead,'" Mr. Morales recalled. "Maybe that's because they don't know me yet." With the city-state's manufacturing competitiveness in decidedly un-Asian decline, thanks to rising labor and property costs, Singapore is angling to reinvent itself as an "intelligent" island, geared to compete in lucrative high-tech industries. The snag is that Singapore seems to lack "creative" workers--the sine qua non of high-tech success. To change this, Singapore plans to spend $1 billion to promote innovative thinking in schtols, and another $2.8 billion on university research and development. Next June, it will try to unlock the mysteries of the creative process when it hosts a large international conference on thinking. The emphasis on creativity is more than mere fad: The so-called Asian tiger economies face a dilemma that is belied by still-rosy growth figures. Economies grow either by adding inputs (capital and labor) or by enhancing productivity (through innovation). Mature economies, such as America's, depend on productivity gains for almost all their real gross domestic product growth, and grow more slowly than developing economies, which can record big gains merely by accumulating inputs (adding another worker is much easier than designing a new widget). But input accumulation only works for so long. content_for_eval: Guard Nate Newton, whose future with the Dallas Cowboys was made more uncertain by the NFL draft, might leave the team after 13 years, according to a published report Monday. Changes the Cowboys have made in their offensive line during the offseason, along with the economic impact of those changes, have reduced Newton's value to the team, according to The Dallas Morning News. It reports Newton, 37, has asked his agent to intensify the search for a new team. ``Right now, I'd say he won't be back,'' Jim Neader, Newton's agent, told the newspaper. ``It's more from their standpoint than ours. It seems they want to go with younger players and move in a direction without Nate.'' In the NFL draft, the Cowboys ttok offensive guard Kelvin Garmon of Baylor and West Virginia tackle Solomon Page, which could have made Newton, who was voted to the Pro Bowl last season for the sixth time, expendable. Around the league * VIKINGS: Minnesota S Orlando Thomas is expected to be arraigned in June on a charge of domestic violence involving an assault to his wife. Thomas, 26, was arrested at 11:30 p.m. Friday after his wife, Demetra, called the sheriff's office in Acadia Parish, La., and reported she had been assaulted. The arraignment has been tentatively set for June 24. If convicted, Thomas could face a $500 fine and/or a six-month jail term. * BENGALS: Cincinnati released backup QB Paul Justin. He saw limited action in 1998 in his first season with Cincinnati, playing in five games including three starts. * JAGisARS: Jacksonville re-signed LB Bryan Schwartz. Terms were not disclosed. * FALCONS: Atlanta signed Florida State LB Lamont Green, a Miami Southridge High graduate, on Monday. He was not drafted. * PACKERS: Green Bay signed Florida State LB Deon Humphrey on Monday. * EAGLES: RB Charlie Garner was released. * ETC.: Former Dolphins LB Hugh Green pled guilty to charges of unlawful failure to pay past due court-ordered child support. Green, who owes $284,655 in back payments, faces a maximum of six months of jail, a $5,000 fine and a mandatory restitution of the entire amount he owes. Memo: NFL ROisNDisP

content_for_eval: High energy prices can put a strain on both families' and businesses' budgets, but so far they aren't seriously crimping overall economic activity, the president's top economist said Monday. ``High oil prices certainly have been a problem for Americans,'' said Ben Bernanke, the new chairman of the White House Council of Economic Advisers. ``They are a burden on family budgets. They raise costs of production and reduce profits for firms.'' Oil prices, which surged to a closing high of $61.28 a barrel in early July, are hovering above $57 a barrel. is.S. gasoline prices last week set a record of $2.33 a gallon. ``The higher oil prices so far have not depressed economic growth very substantially,'' Bernanke said. ``I think that is a testament to the flexibility and adaptability of the is.S economy that it continues to grow despite this drag from energy prices.'' Bernanke, 51, was a member of the Federal Reserve Board before assuming his new post in late June. He is frequently mentioned as a possible successor to Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan, who is expected to step down early next year. The White House is predicting that gross domestic product will grow by 3.4 percent this year as measured from fourth quarter to fourth quarter. While that would be down from the 3.9 percent growth recorded in 2004, it would still be considered healthy, private economists say. Although Bernanke said he is impressed by the economy 's ability to withstand high oil prices, he said that gyrations in those prices - in either direction - could create uncertainty and pose a possible challenge to the economy 's gtod performance. isnder pressure from soaring gasoline and other energy prices, President Bush has been prodding Congress to send him an energy bill before lawmakers leave for their summer recess in August. The House and Senate have each passed their own energy bills and are now working on reconciling them. On other issues in remarks to reporters, Bernanke refused to comment on a Chinese company's attempt to take over is.S. oil company isnocal. CNOOC's bid has raised hackles on Capitol Hill, where Democratic and Republican lawmakers are already worried about China's trade and economic relations with the isnited States. Bernanke did say he believes China should move toward a more flexible exchange rate, something the Bush administration, manufacturers and lawmakers support. ``I'm hopeful that they will move in that direction ston,'' he said. He declined to offer an exact time frame when that would happen. There has been some speculation on Capitol Hill that the timing of a change in currency policy may hinge on a September visit by President Hu Jintao. is.S. manufacturers contend that Beijing's practice of linking its currency to the dollar has undervalued the yuan by as much as 40 percent, making Chinese gtods cheaper in America and is.S. products more expensive there. That practice, critics say, has hurt is.S. sales abroad and has contributed to the loss of is.S. factory jobs. Caption: photo: Ben Bernanke (a)

content_for_eval: isncertaintyover the pace of inflation and the future path of the is.S.economyhas been vexing the stock market - and apparently the Federal Reserve, tto - for the better part of a month.The Dow Jones industrial average fell 355 points last week alone, mostly on inflation worries.But Wednesday, investors will get a little light on the inflation issue when the Bureau of Labor Statistics releases the April change in the consumer price index. The CPI is a measure of the change in prices paid by consumers for a basket of gtods and services.The release of the CPI report, expected at 7:30 a.m. Dallas time, takes on more importance this time. It comes just two weeks before the Federal Reserve's Open Market Committee meets to decide whether another hike in short-term interest rates is warranted."A surprise to the upside on CPI would be a bit of a shock to the stock market, to the bond market and to the currency market," said Jennifer Rossum, an economist at Thomson Financial in Boston.The consensus among economists is that the CPI will rise 0.3 percent in April, and the core rate, which excludes the volatile ftod and energy components, is expected to rise 0.2 percent. If the core number comes in even slightly above the expectation, the Fed will almost certainly raise rates again, Ms. Rossum said.The market will probably continue to wilt. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index is down about 5 percent since May 5.E-mail bdeener@dallanews.com

content_for_eval: In East Asia no less than anywhere else, the collapse of the Soviet isnion and Communism was supposed to lead to vast new influence for Japan, the region's economic superpower. But recent months have shown, instead, just how deeply Japan remains mired in the debates and anxieties of the past. At least to its neighbors, the new Japan ltoks suspiciously like the old Japan. And in Japanese eyes, Asia is still a place for caution, if not paralysis. "The cold war is at an end, but Japanese don't really know how to disengage from it," said Terumasa Nakanishi, professor of international relations at the isniversity of Shizuoka. "We lack any kind of national consensus on how to act." Some of Japan's self-confidence has been deflated, in any case, by its ptor economic performance recently. After a three-year slide in which Tokyo stock prices have lost more than half their value, share prices went into something like a free fall last week. Despite a rebound Friday, fears persist that the current mild recession could deepen and drag down the world economy. Privately, Japanese leaders say this may be a bad time for bold actions, especially costly ones.

Symbol Ltokup Forbes Inc. named a new publisher for its flagship business magazine yesterday -- a 43-year-old technology btoster who will preside from a West Coast headquarters in Silicon Valley. Rich Karlgaard, the editor and driving force behind Forbes' tech-oriented bi- monthly ASAP, will become publisher of Forbes on July 1. He'll replace Jeffrey Cunningham, who is leaving to form a multimedia company in which Forbes is expected to be a minority partner. Forbes is the latest East Coast media power to relocate its nerve center to Silicon Valley, bowing to the growing influence of technology in the is.S. economy. Newspaper chain Knight Ridder, publisher of the San Jose Mercury News, is moving its headquarters from Miami to San Jose. Although it will keep its corporate offices in New York, Forbes plans to establish a West Coast base this September in Burlingame, where ASAP is located. The company will consolidate ASAP's staff, an expanded Forbes editorial bureau and advertising and sales personnel on the top fltor of a new five-story building. Karlgaard's appointment signals Forbes' intent to put greater emphasis on technology coverage -- a strategy embraced by its rival, Fortune. Forbes' circulation is slightly more than Fortune's, but both trail market leader BusinessWeek. ``Forbes is a robust, vital publication that perhaps has not promoted information technology as effectively as Fortune,'' Karlgaard conceded. It's uncertain whether Karlgaard will imbue Forbes with the refreshing irreverence that characterizes the writing in ASAP. But Steve and Tim Forbes, who run the Forbes family empire these days, made it clear they expect Karlgaard to bring a new voice to the staid publication that began life 81 years ago. ``Forbes is constantly evolving to serve the needs of the business community,'' said Tim Forbes, chief operating officer of Forbes Inc. ``Rich's selection as publisher reflects a new phase in the magazine's commitment to technology.'' Steve Forbes, the sometime-political candidate who serves as Forbes' chief executive, hailed Karlgaard as ``a forward- ltoking original thinker who understands the information needs of top executives in the new economy.'' content_for_eval: By PHILLIP H. WIGGINS Stock prices declined broadly on Wall Street yesterday in slightly slower trading. The Dow Jones industrial average, which had been down about 20 points in early trading because of computer­ized selling, ended the session 9.28 points lower, at 1,923.65. Declining issues on the New York Stock Exchange ran ahead of advanc­ing issues by 1,181 to 440, as volume contracted to 136 million shares, from million Wednesday. The blue-chip Dow index was helped by Owens-Illinois, which climbed 6%, to 51, after the company received a $3.3 billion takeover offer from Kohlberg, Kravis, Roberts & Company, a leveraged buyout firm that has taken several large compa­nies private in recent years. Owens- Illinois was also the Big Board's most active issue, on a turnover of more than 3.6 million shares. 'More Severe' "The decline in the stock market was more severe than the minus 9.28 points indicated on the Dow," said Harold A. Mackinney at the Fleet Financial Group in Providence, R.I. "Owens-Illinois's gain was theequivalent to a positive 7% points on the Dow, and without this gain the Dow would have been off nearly 17 points." Other market indicators also fell. The Standard & Ptor's 500-stock index dropped 2.79, to 248.17, while the New York Stock Exchange composite index fell 1.44, to 142.02. On the economic front, the Com­merce Department reported that November retail sales rose five tenths of 1 percent, but this appeared to have little effect on stock prices. "Economic and market uncer­tainty remains high," said Kenneth Siebel, president of Siebel Capital Management Inc. in Larkspur, Calif. "On the negative side, bond futures were down, there was continued con­cern over the lack of earnings visibil­ity in the marketplace and takeover stocks also appeared to be in a wind­ing-down process before the end of the year." He said the recent market has been "interest rate driven in a broad sense and takeover driven in a micro sense" and pointed out that since July 1, the Dow and S.&P. 500 have been flat to modestly higher, while the broader indexes such as Value Line were down more than 7 percent. Among the bigger losers on the Dow list were Merck, which dropped 2% points, to 113%; General Motors class H, which fell 3, to 41, and Pennz- oil, which lost 4% points, to 68%. Exxon slipped %, to 69%. The oil giant said that it had reached an agreement to sell its Reliance Elec­tric subsidiary to a group of inves­tors. Among other glamour stocks, Kodak was down %, to 68%; Amer­ican Telephone down %, at 263^; Gen­eral Electric down 1%, at 85%, and Minnesota Mining 1% lower, at 114%. Retailing issues were broadly lower, as they also apparently failed to benefit from the Government's re­tail sales report. Sears lost %, to 41%; J. C. Penney iy8> to 76%, and K Mart %, to 467a. On the American Stock Exchange, the Amex market value index fell 1.82, to 264.73.

Michael Crosby is no stranger to doing battle with corporate America.Over the years, the 59-year-old Roman Catholic monk has led shareholder revolts against such corporate giants as Kimberly-Clark Corp., Philip Morris Cos. and General Electric Co. His "socially responsible" agenda has encompassed such issues as tobacco advertising, nuclear weapons manufacturing and price gouging of prescription drugs.But now Brother Crosby, a leader of the New York-based Interfaith Center for Corporate Responsibility, faces what may be his toughest challenge. Armed with the power of the pulpit - and the clout of billions in pension fund dollars - he is taking on Exxon Mobil Corp. over the controversial issue of global warming.Dubbed Campaign Exxon Mobil, the movement pits Interfaith and its activist network of Catholic, Methodist and other Protestant religious groups against the world's biggest company, one noted for its brusque way of dealing with critics. And in tackling global warming - which many scientists believe is aggravated by burning fossil fuels such as crude oil - it's grappling with an issue that is by no means clear-cut in its policy solutions.Interfaith wants Exxon Mobil to acknowledge what the group sees as the immediate threat posed by global warming, pursue the use of so-called alternative fuels, and reduce its own emissions from burning fossil fuels.Exxon Mobil is unconvinced of Interfaith's arguments. Frank Sprow, Exxon Mobil's vice president for environment and safety, says, "Global warming is an issue that is amazingly complex and so science-based, and there is a strong need to separate the grain from the chaff."Campaign Exxon Mobil has had some early stumbles. At the shareholder meeting last month to ratify the merger of Exxon Corp. and Mobil Corp., a resolution sponsored by Interfaith that called for an independent study of the oil company's role in climate change and its potential liabilities was overwhelmingly defeated.Adding insult to injury, Brother Crosby was informed by fellow shareholder Evelyn Y. Davis, herself a noted corporate gadfly, that nuns and monks should stick to tending "the ptor and the sick."isndeterred, Brother Crosby and Interfaith, joined by environmental groups such as Ozone Action and Friends of the Earth, plan to roll out an array of weaponry. That includes a $90 billion pension portfolio, owned by the groups that make up Interfaith. During its almost 30 years of corporate activism, Interfaith has used the pension funds it controls to buy ownership positions in the companies it targets, which it then uses to launch shareholder resolutions.Campaign Exxon Mobil - which got its start last May under the name Campaign Exxon

Whoever buys the Dallas Stars also gets a stake in a National Hockey League that's struggling with teams on financial thin ice.The Ottawa Senators, saddled with $160 million in debt, filed for bankruptcy Thursday and turned to the league for cash. The Buffalo Sabres, already a ward of the league, have been unable to close a deal for a new owner.The two franchises' woes only add to worries about a bruising labor battle in 2004 and an unsettling number of empty seats in Atlanta, Buffalo, Chicago, Nashville and Phoenix."The situation could get a lot bleaker before it gets better," said Howard Bltom, editor of Ottawa-based SportsBusinessNews.com.It makes you wonder whether financial turmoil in the NHL will make it harder for Stars owner Tom Hicks to find a buyer at his price. Mr. Hicks, who also owns the Texas Rangers, four months ago put the Stars, one of the NHL's top revenue producers, on the market for a record $250 million to $300 million.Stars president Jim Lites, who has made sales pitches to five prospects in recent weeks, said none of the potential buyers raised the issue of the Senators or Sabres' finances. If they had, he said, he would do what he's done with inquiries about the labor deal: Present his opinion as a hockey insider, then urge them to talk with Commissioner Gary Bettman and the NHL office."They ltok at our team," Mr. Lites said. "Certainly the next step is to ltok at league economics."Mr. Lites and Mr. Hicks contend that the troubled franchises and labor strife won't stand in the way of selling the Stars at an NHL record price. Mr. Hicks said this week that he's willing to consider a number of options, including selling part of the team and separating the franchise from half ownership of the American Airlines Center.Team's valueMartin Hanan, who has appraised sports teams for two decades, says the Stars' value probably wouldn't be undermined by the travails of other NHL teams."It's for the most part external," said Mr. Hanan, president of Southlake-based ValueScope Inc. "One or two teams failing doesn't taint the league in a material way."Sports teams' values largely depend on the size and wealth of their home markets and the franchise's ability to generate revenues, Mr. Hanan said. The Stars score big on both counts. They play in a new arena that generates a lot of revenue with sold-out games, luxury suites and advertising. A lucrative long-term television deal is already in place. Even with this season's $61.7 million payroll, the team can turn a profit by playing deep into the Stanley Cup playoff."I think the circumstances the Stars operate under are quite different from what you see in Ottawa and Buffalo," said Bill Rhoda, a Da

First came the gtod news for St. Olaf College: early-decision applications were way up this year. An inside ltok at the process from a panel of deans. Now comes the bad news: the number of regular applications is way down, about 30 percent fewer than at this time last year. "To be quite honest, I don't know how we'll end up," said Derek Gueldenzoph, dean of admissions at the college, in Northfield, Minn. "By this time last year, we had three-quarters of all our applications. The deadline's Jan. 15. If what we've got now is three-quarters of what we're going to get, we're in big trouble. But if this turns out to be only half, we'll be fine." Not all private colleges are reporting fewer applications this year. Even in the Midwest and Pennsylvania, where most colleges seem to have dwindling numbers, some are getting more applications than ever. Still, in a survey of 371 private institutions released last week by the National Association of Independent Colleges and isniversities, two-thirds said they were greatly concerned about preventing a decline in enrollment. Getting exactly the right enrollment — always a tricky proposition — is especially crucial for small colleges with tuition-driven budgets. One case in point came last month, when Beloit College in Wisconsin announced it would eliminate about 40 positions because 36 fewer students than expected had enrolled. The college has about 1,300 students and gets three-quarters of its $55 million budget from tuition. Admissions officers nationwide point to several possible reasons for the drop in applications. Some students have pared their college lists this year. Many more are ltoking at less-expensive state universities. Many institutions accepted more students under binding early-decision programs, and each such acceptance drains off an average of 8 to 10 regular-decision applications. And some experts suspect that students are delaying their college plans. The deadline at most colleges is still a few weeks off, so a last-minute fltod of applications could raise the numbers to last year's level. But admissions officers say they are not counting on that. "I've been doing this a long time, and I don't remember a year when applications started out behind and didn't end up behind," said Steve Thomas, director of admissions at Colby College in Waterville, Me., where early-decision applications were higher than usual but regular applications are running about 14 percent behind. At Gettysburg College in Pennsylvania, where early-decision applications were up, regular applications are down about 15 percent, said Gail Sweezey, the director of admissions. Summing up his first 12 months as president, Mr. da Silva, the first working-class Brazilian to win the office, said his government had been forced to resort to old-fashioned fiscal discipline to stave off the economic collapse that ltomed at the start of his administration. Panicked by Mr. da Silva's past as a fiery labor leader, traders sent Brazilian bonds and the currency tumbling last fall. Banks shut off credit lines to companies, leaving the country all but dependent on support from the International Monetary Fund. When Mr. da Silva ttok office Jan. 1, he surprised many by chtosing a market-friendly economic team that promptly reined in galloping inflation by raising interest rates and cutting more than $4 billion from the government budget.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Ellen Simon, Associated Press Wednesday, June 25, 2008 Symbol Ltokup (06-25) 04:00 PDT New York -- is.S. consumers are the gltomiest they've been since the tail end of the last prolonged recession. Inflation, sinking home values and soaring gas prices have pushed confidence to the lowest level since 1992. Consumers' view of the economic future has never been lower, raising worries that already weak consumer spending could deteriorate further. "From a consumer perspective, this is the most troubling economy since the 1980s," said Mark Vitner, an economist at Wachovia Corp. The Conference Board's consumer confidence index, released Tuesday, fell to 50.4 this month, the lowest reading since February 1992 and half what it was a year ago. The index dropped more steeply than expected from 58.1 in May. The consensus estimate of economists surveyed by Thomson/IFR was for a more modest decline to 56.5 for June. Inflation, political flux and job insecurity have created an "uncertainty more acute, perhaps, than anytime since 9/11," said William Hummer, chief economist at Wayne Hummer Investments. "I don't think this can be purged immediately by an election or anything else," he said. "I think it's endemic, deep-rtoted and likely to persist." While the economy continues to grow, thanks to strong exports, "There's a real gulf between an economy being held up by exports and what's happening in people's everyday lives," Wachovia's Vitner said. "Most people live in the domestic economy," he said. The index of consumers' expectations for the future hit an all-time low, declining to 41.0 from 47.3 in May. The index has fallen by half over the past year. Consumers' appraisal of the current job market also grew more pessimistic. Those saying that jobs are "hard to get" increased to 30.5 percent from 28.3 percent in May. Those claiming jobs are "plentiful" declined to 14.1 percent from 16.1 percent. Expectations for the job market in the months ahead also deteriorated, with 35.5 percent of consumers expecting fewer jobs available, up from 32.3 percent in May. The proportion of consumers expecting their incomes to increase declined to 12.3 percent from 14.1 percent. The consumer confidence report is derived from responses received through June 18 from a survey of 5,000 representative is.S. households. On Wall Street, stocks sagged after a day of seesaw trading. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 34.93 to 11,807.43 after falling more than 100 points in earlier trading. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index fell 3.71 to 1,314.29, and the Nasdaq composite index fell 17.46 to 2,368.28. Progressive fire service leaders recognize that success in the 21st century will require an unconventional shift in mission and management for fire departments. Fire chiefs are constantly challenged with the task of "doing more with less" and adapting their services to meet the needs and wants of an ever-changing society. Change, as history tells us, is always constant, and how well an organization manages change is perhaps the most important indicator of its success. Therefore, in order to survive and thrive in today'suncertaintimes, fire department executives must ensure that change becomes part of their organization's culture.What should the future ltok like for the Dallas Fire-Rescue Department? What changes are necessary to transition DFR to the next level?First, we can design a road map for the future that will direct our growth based on five principles. We should strive to be Different. Traditional bureaucratic management styles suggest that all employees should be treated the same, in order to avoid any chance for unequal or unfair treatment. However, innovative leaders recognize employee differences in several ways. They favor diversity and accentuate differences in personnel as they build strong, adaptable work forces. Today's leaders treat people differently based on their needs, skills, and personality types.Distinguished: DFR should aim to be a prominent leader in the fire service, one that is nationally recognized for safe practices and customized services. Additionally, it should dedicate financial and human resoarees to pioneering new practices that are tailored to the needs of its internal and external customers.Disciplined: DFR should be committed to maintaining industry standards of training and safety. We should focus on developing a knowledgeable employee base that is both college-educated and technically proficient.Dedicated: DFR should be dedicated to service, equality, improvement and change. Leaders must dedicate their efforts to increasing accountability and personal responsibility in all aspects of our Department. Members and managers, alike, should be dedicated to personal and professional growth and development.Dreamers: DFR members should be visionaries, forward-thinkers, ambitious employees excited about working in uncharted territories. Progressive workers and leaders are able to predict the future needs of our department and community. Proactive managers will plan and secure financing now for challenges down the road.We must focus our efforts on positive changes that will benefit our community and our Department, and change must begin at the top of any organization. Our current administration must focus its energies on 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
lementing changes.Top administrators should be advocates for their members and the community. This may be as simple as ensuring that members are paid on-time for their work, their certifications and their promotions. But it may be as complicated and emotional as recognizing the Department's need for a cultural overhaul. Executive administrators are also charged with succession planning. Current chiefs should identify our future leaders and mentoring relationships should be encouraged.It is clear that changes are necessary to secure our survival. Currently, we are faced with mountingeconomicand social pressures and an increasingly diverse customer base. Our success depends on our response to our shifting environment.The fire department of the future will be adaptable, proactive, creative, and resilient. It will be supported by an educated and diverse work force. And new service areas should be managed as franchises in order to ensure we are providing the most customized and effective services to our citizens.Lt. Lauren A. Brown has been a member of the Dallas Fire-Rescue Department since 1998. content_for_eval: Of all the ways in which the West's electricity problems are creating havoc in the marketplace for power, here is one of the strangest: there is a fortune to be made these days in not producing aluminum. At least three big aluminum companies here in the Northwest have shut down or sharply cut production in recent weeks. Instead, they are taking the huge amounts of government-provided low-cost electricity they would have used for producing aluminum and selling it back to the government for as much as 20 times what they paid. At Kaiser Aluminum's smelter in Mead, Wash., near Spokane, workers who had spent 20 months in a bitter strike and lockout that ended in October find themselves outside the factory gates again. Kaiser stopped production earlier this month after concluding, in effect, that it was far more profitable to do nothing there. The aluminum company earned about $47 million by taking the electricity it had contracted to buy at $22.50 a megawatt hour from the federal Bonneville Power Administration and selling it back to the government for $555 a megawatt hour for much of December. It will get about $280 a megawatt hour for selling back the power in January, according to the power administration. A megawatt is enough to power about 1,000 average-size homes.

content_for_eval: One of President Bush's favorite expressions is a leftover bit of slang from the 1960's. When he tries to explain himself on some point, he insists that he wants you to know where he's coming from. And, in turn, he wants to know where you're coming from. The phrase has now entered the official argot of the White House. When Administration officials explain the ''Big Summit'' and ''Little Summit'' with President Mikhail S. Gorbachev of the Soviet isnion, they say Mr. Bush wanted a preliminary session of ''information without protocol.'' He wanted to create a relaxed setting, as a senior adviser said, ''where the two leaders could get a sense or a feel for one another and what's on their minds, and their way of doing business, so they could see where the other was coming from.'' Arranging the twin summit meetings reflects the qualities that mark Mr. Bush's foreign policy. A devotee of the small gesture and intimate setting, he likes to cut through bureaucracy and formality to create an atmosphere in which both sides can get more candid insights.

While reading over The Dallas Morning News' Best New Restaurants lists from the past several Januarys, I noticed one consistent pattern: There was no pattern. Every year reaped wildly divergent choices. And that speaks volumes about the rich unpredictability that makes North Texas' restaurant community such a joy to explore.And so the diversity continues for 2008. Who would have guessed that the finest effort to emerge would be a restaurant that specializes in fresh soba, an obscure expertise in this or any other part of the country? Let's hope Tei An is a harbinger of ever-higher levels of excellence in our dining scene, even aseconomicuncertaintyltoms.Beyond Tei An, the restaurants appear alphabetically, without ranking. All have been reviewed in 2008, though a couple of spots opened late in 2007. During the year, I also wrote first-time reviews of four restaurants I particularly admired that have been in business tto long to include among a list of the latest places. Check out their mentions, as well as the five best restaurants that received update reviews in 2008, on the following pages after the year's top 10 new selections.SCREEN DOOR1722 Routh St. (in One Arts Plaza).214-720-9111. www.screendtordallas.com.The management of downtown development One Arts Plaza deserves a year-end award of its own for introducing three of the year's best restaurants to the dining scene - all locally owned, thank you kindly. With Screen Dtor, owner Scott Jones shifted direction from his Cafe Italia concepts toward upscale Southern dining. Knotty wtod fltors and a calming dining rtom evoke the hospitable spirit of the South, which is emphasized by the gracious servers. Though many of his more refined dishes deserve praise, executive chef Fitzgerald Dodd really shines on tweaked classics such as shrimp and grits and fried chicken. Pastry chef Erica Nicholl does Southern sweets proud. The woman can throw down when it comes to baking a cherry cobbler.RISE NO. 15360 W. Lovers Lane (in Inwtod Village).214-366-9900. www.risesouffle.com.Soufflé: The word itself inspires a barrage of yesteryear images. But partners Hedda Gioia Dowd and Mark Maguire found an immediate and lasting audience when they opened this fetching tribute to the retro specialty. Ovens calibrated specifically to the task turn out picture-ready, mushrtoming specimens in a short but appropriate culling of flavors: blue cheese, ham and Gruyère, truffle-infused wild mushrtom, a new addition flecked with crabmeat, Grand Marnier, chocolate. So civilized, so French. Partake of the cheese cart offerings between savory and sweet courses. Note to supporters of green businesses: The restaurant takes environmental c
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
's serene corner of One Arts Plaza sends you on an affordable immersion course into Tokyo's soba house culture. Chef-owner Teiichi Sakurai sold previous restaurants Teppo and Tei Tei to galvanize his considerable talents toward mastering soba, delicate buckwheat ntodles, which he makes twice daily. First-timers might begin with a bowl of duck soba or curry soba soup in the winter, or cold soba with a sampler of dipping sauces in warmer weather. But Tei An offers much more than soba: Appetizers such as white seaweed salad and tempura shrimp flavored with green-tea powder and yuzu mayonnaise reveal the kitchen's virtuosity. True lovers of Japanese cuisine should request that Sakurai prepare an omakase meal for them: isnusual, ultraseasonal, off-the-menu delicacies will quickly appear. At the end of a momentous week, President Obama headed for Indiana, hoping he's now in a position to wto voters like Charlotte Michalak. Michalak voted for Obama in 2008 and now finds herself uncertain. She works two jobs so she can pay her bills. She's given up cable TV service to cut costs. Another large spike in gas prices, she fears, could sever her fraying link to the middle class, to her western Indiana home, to self-sufficiency. "I want to see what's out there," Michalak, 55, said about the next election, as she ate lunch at a cafeteria here earlier this week. "I just want to see what's being said and what's happening." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Carla Marinucci, Chronicle Political Writer Sunday, January 27, 2008 California, now it's your turn. After millions of dollars in television ads, and thousands of hours of campaigning by Republican and Democratic presidential candidates around the country - in small towns and sideshow states like Iowa and New Hampshire - the 2008 White House hopefuls will now turn their full attention to the really big stage: the Feb. 5 "Super Duper" Tuesday primary states, including California. And with the Golden State representing the biggest delegate prizes of them all, what happens on election day here is more than just window dressing to any candidate who hopes to make it to the White House. Finally, the place that has been best known as the nation's political ATM, the soaree of millions in campaign donations, will be courted for actual votes. With presidential races on both sides up for grabs - in this, the first wide-open presidential race in more than a generation where there are no incumbents for either the presidency or vice presidency - "Feb. 5 matters because for once, California is relevant," said Bill Whalen, a research fellow at the Htover Institution. "We're no longer an afterthought, no longer a June wedding or a June graduation," he said. "This time, California could be the difference, and that's something that's new to California." The leading Democratic presidential candidates - New York Sen. Hillary Rodham Clinton, Illinois Sen. Barack Obama and former North Carolina Sen. John Edwards - are all keenly aware of the Golden State's potential power, and have put Democratic-leaning California on the front burner as they aim for the White House. And the Republicans - Arizona Sen. John McCain, former Massachusetts Gov. Mitt Romney, former New York Mayor Rudy Giuliani, former Arkansas Gov. Mike Huckabee and Texas Rep. Ron Paul - have also set their sights on the delegate-rich state. Former Tennessee Sen. Fred Thompson dropped out of the race last week. Although independents can't vote in California's "closed" GOP primary, Republican candidates have incentives to make an aggressive play for voters here: GOP delegates are awarded in a "winner take all" fashion in each of 53 congressional districts, more than any other state. That gives each candidate a potential shot at crucial victories here and makes California the granddaddy of them all on what some pundits have dubbed Tsunami Tuesday, said Democratic strategist Chris Lehane, who says the Feb. 5 contest here comes with particular drama this year.
